revision.comment: from the existing Japanese Wikipedia article at [[:ja:勧請縄]]; see its history for attribution.

revision.text:
'''Kanjo Nawa'''（{{lang-ja|勧請縄}}）は、村境（地域・地区の域内・外のさかい）に、呪物を付した[[注連縄]]を張る習慣。[[道切り]]とも呼ばれる。
日本全国の農村地域では、疫病などの災厄が域内に入るのを防いだり、災厄を賦与して域内から追い出す意味合いで、藁や草で作った縄、人形、わらじなどを村境に祀る例が残る<ref>印南敏秀「村落の境界呪物 ―南山城を中心とした近畿のカンジョウナワを事例として―」『民俗文化分布圏論』 1993年（平成5年）12月28日、375-376頁、ISBN 4-626-01486-0</ref><ref>高嶋賢二「村境の大草履―四国西南部の事例を中心に」日本民具学会『民具研究 (131)』、2005年3月</ref><ref>小口千明「農村集落における精神的ムラ境の諸相 ―茨城県桜村における虫送りと道切りを事例として―」（城西人文研究第12号 昭和62年（1987年）2月10日）</ref>。[[近畿地方]]周辺では、[[福井県]][[若狭国|若狭地方]]、[[滋賀県]]湖東・湖南地方、[[三重県]][[伊賀国|伊賀地方]]、[[奈良県]]東部山間部、[[京都府]]南山城地域などにまとまって分布しており<ref>印南敏秀「村落の境界呪物」、P.377 カンジョウナワの分布図</ref>、勧請縄の他、'''勧請吊'''（かんじょうつり）という呼称もある<ref>原田敏丸「近江の勧請吊」『彦根論叢』第48・49号・人文科学特集第9号合併 昭和33年（1958年）、p.345-360</ref>。
== 各地の勧請縄 == *飛鳥路の勧請縄行事（[[京都府]][[相楽郡]][[笠置町]]飛鳥路区 無形民俗文化財 平成10年（1998年）登録） *:飛鳥路区でおこなわれている勧請縄行事は、村内の招福や安全を祈るため、毎年1月初旬に各戸より1人ずつ出て注連縄を作り、集落を南北に流れる布目川に渡す行事である。注連縄には農具や生活用品、また男根や女陰をかたどった藁製のツクリモノを下げる<ref>「飛鳥路の勧請縄行事」京都の文化財第16集 平成11年（1999年）3月 京都府教育委員会</ref>。 <gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄01.jpg|笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄 File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄02.jpg|the same File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|the same File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄04.jpg|the same File: AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|笠置町天照御門神社の舞台。勧請縄の作成はここで行われる。 </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * [[Shimenawa]] * {{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り}} - Similar custom
== External links == * [http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452 Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)] - explanation by the local government of [[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]
[[:Category:Japanese folk religion]] {{culture-stub}}

'''Kanjo Nawa'''（{{lang-ja|勧請縄}}） is a custom of stretching a [[Shimenawa]] with [[Fetishism|fetishes]] hung at the border of a village. It is also know as {{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り}}(道切り).
日本全国の農村地域では、疫病などの災厄が域内に入るのを防いだり、災厄を賦与して域内から追い出す意味合いで、藁や草で作った縄、人形、わらじなどを村境に祀る例が残る<ref>印南敏秀「村落の境界呪物 ―南山城を中心とした近畿のカンジョウナワを事例として―」『民俗文化分布圏論』 1993年（平成5年）12月28日、375-376頁、ISBN 4-626-01486-0</ref><ref>高嶋賢二「村境の大草履―四国西南部の事例を中心に」日本民具学会『民具研究 (131)』、2005年3月</ref><ref>小口千明「農村集落における精神的ムラ境の諸相 ―茨城県桜村における虫送りと道切りを事例として―」（城西人文研究第12号 昭和62年（1987年）2月10日）</ref>。[[近畿地方]]周辺では、[[福井県]][[若狭国|若狭地方]]、[[滋賀県]]湖東・湖南地方、[[三重県]][[伊賀国|伊賀地方]]、[[奈良県]]東部山間部、[[京都府]]南山城地域などにまとまって分布しており<ref>印南敏秀「村落の境界呪物」、P.377 カンジョウナワの分布図</ref>、勧請縄の他、'''勧請吊'''（かんじょうつり）という呼称もある<ref>原田敏丸「近江の勧請吊」『彦根論叢』第48・49号・人文科学特集第9号合併 昭和33年（1958年）、p.345-360</ref>。
== Examples == *Event of Kanjo Nawa in Asukaji (Kasukaji, [[Kasagi, Kyoto]]) *:飛鳥路区でおこなわれている勧請縄行事は、村内の招福や安全を祈るため、毎年1月初旬に各戸より1人ずつ出て注連縄を作り、集落を南北に流れる布目川に渡す行事である。注連縄には農具や生活用品、また男根や女陰をかたどった藁製のツクリモノを下げる。無形民俗文化財 平成10年（1998年）登録） <ref>「飛鳥路の勧請縄行事」京都の文化財第16集 平成11年（1999年）3月 京都府教育委員会</ref><nowiki>{{。</nowiki> <gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄01.jpg|Kanjo Nawa in Aasukaji File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄02.jpg|the same File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|the same File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄04.jpg|the same File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|笠置町天照御門神社の舞台。勧請縄の作成はここで行われる。 </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * [[Shimenawa]] * {{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り}} - Similar custom
== External links == * [http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452 Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)] - explanation by the local government of [[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]
[[:Category:Japanese folk religion]] {{culture-stub}}

'''Kanjo Nawa'''（{{lang-ja|勧請縄}}） is a custom of stretching a [[Shimenawa]] with [[Fetishism|fetishes]] hung at the border of a village. It is also know as {{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り}}(道切り).
日本全国の農村地域では、疫病などの災厄が域内に入るのを防いだり、災厄を賦与して域内から追い出す意味合いで、藁や草で作った縄、人形、わらじなどを村境に祀る例が残る<ref name="KOTOBANK">{{cite web| url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8B%A7%E8%AB%8B%E7%B8%84-1295507 | title=勧請縄(かんじょうなわ)とは - コトバンク | publisher={{ill|kotobank|ja|コトバンク}} | accessdate=2017-08-11 | language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|author=印南敏秀|editors= 植木行宣, 樋口昭|title=民俗文化分布圏論|date=1993-12-28|pages=375-376|isbn=4-626-01486-0}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=高嶋賢二|title=村境の大草履―四国西南部の事例を中心に|publisher=日本民具学会|journal=民具研究 |volume=131|year=2005|pages=45-61}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=小口千明|title=農村集落における精神的ムラ境の諸相 ―茨城県桜村における虫送りと道切りを事例として―|journal=城西人文研究|volume=12|pages=37-51|url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/els/contents110000187832.pdf?id=ART0000550707|date=1987-02-10}}</ref>。[[近畿地方]]周辺では、[[福井県]][[若狭国|若狭地方]]、[[滋賀県]]湖東・湖南地方、[[三重県]][[伊賀国|伊賀地方]]、[[奈良県]]東部山間部、[[京都府]]南山城地域などにまとまって分布しており<ref>{{cite book|author=印南敏秀|editors= 植木行宣, 樋口昭|title=民俗文化分布圏論|date=1993-12-28|pages=377|isbn=4-626-01486-0}}</ref>、勧請縄の他、'''勧請吊'''（かんじょうつり）という呼称もある<ref>{{cite journal|author=原田敏丸|title=近江の勧請吊 彦根論叢 | volume=48/49 | journal=人文科学特集第9号合併 | year=1958 | pages=345-360 | url=http://libdspace.biwako.shiga-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10441/7726/2/HIKONE%20RONSO_048-049_345-360Y%20harada.pdf }}</ref>。
== Examples == *Event of Kanjo Nawa in Asukaji (Kasukaji, [[Kasagi, Kyoto]]) *:飛鳥路区でおこなわれている勧請縄行事は、村内の招福や安全を祈るため、毎年1月初旬に各戸より1人ずつ出て注連縄を作り、集落を南北に流れる布目川に渡す行事である。注連縄には農具や生活用品、また男根や女陰をかたどった藁製のツクリモノを下げる。無形民俗文化財 1998年登録） <ref>"飛鳥路の勧請縄行事" 京都の文化財第16集 March 1999 京都府教育委員会</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|accessdate=2017-09-11}}</ref>。
<gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄01.jpg|Kanjo Nawa in Aasukaji File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄02.jpg|the same File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|the same File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄04.jpg|the same File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|笠置町天照御門神社の舞台。勧請縄の作成はここで行われる。 </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * [[Shimenawa]] * {{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り}} - Similar custom
== External links == * [http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452 Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)] - explanation by the local government of [[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]
[[:Category:Japanese folk religion]] {{culture-stub}}

'''Kanjo Nawa'''（{{lang-ja|勧請縄}}） is a custom of stretching a [[Shimenawa]] with [[Fetishism|fetishes]] hung at the border of a village. {{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り}}(道切り) is just a similar custom.
In rural area around Japan, there remains the custom of enshrining the items such as ropes of straw and grass, dolls, and straw sandals at the border of the village intended to prevent the evil such as an epidemic from entering the village, or to drive out the evil.<ref name="KOTOBANK">{{cite web| url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8B%A7%E8%AB%8B%E7%B8%84-1295507 | title=勧請縄(かんじょうなわ)とは - コトバンク | publisher={{ill|kotobank|ja|コトバンク}} | accessdate=2017-08-11 | language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|author=印南敏秀|editors= 植木行宣, 樋口昭|title=民俗文化分布圏論|date=1993-12-28|pages=375-376|isbn=4-626-01486-0}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=高嶋賢二|title=村境の大草履―四国西南部の事例を中心に|publisher=日本民具学会|journal=民具研究 |volume=131|year=2005|pages=45-61}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=小口千明|title=農村集落における精神的ムラ境の諸相 ―茨城県桜村における虫送りと道切りを事例として―|journal=城西人文研究|volume=12|pages=37-51|url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/els/contents110000187832.pdf?id=ART0000550707|date=1987-02-10}}</ref>
This custom can be found around [[Kinki Region]], especially in [[Wakasa]], [[Fukui Prefecture]], in the east and south of [[Shiga Prefecture]], in [[Iga, Mie|Iga, Mie Prefecture]], in the east mountainous region of [[Nara Prefecture]], and in Minami-Yamashiro of [[Kyoto Prefecture]]<ref>{{cite book|title=民俗文化分布圏論|author=印南敏秀|date=1993-12-28|isbn=4-626-01486-0|editors=植木行宣, 樋口昭|pages=377}}</ref>. Kanjo Nawa is also known as '''Kanjo Tsuri''' (勧請吊).<ref>{{cite journal|author=原田敏丸|year=1958|title=近江の勧請吊 彦根論叢|url=http://libdspace.biwako.shiga-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10441/7726/2/HIKONE%20RONSO_048-049_345-360Y%20harada.pdf|journal=人文科学特集第9号合併|volume=48/49|pages=345-360}}</ref>
== Examples == *Event of Kanjo Nawa in Asukaji (Kasukaji, [[Kasagi, Kyoto]]) *:In Asukaji, in every January, a man *:鳥路区でおこなわれている勧請縄行事は、村内の招福や安全を祈るため、毎年1月初旬に各戸より1人ずつ出て注連縄を作り、集落を南北に流れる布目川に渡す行事である。 *:Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as [[Cultural Property (Japan)|Search Results Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] in 1998.<ref>"飛鳥路の勧請縄行事" 京都の文化財第16集 March 1999 京都府教育委員会</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|accessdate=2017-09-11}}</ref>
<gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄01.jpg|Kanjo Nawa in Aasukaji File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄02.jpg|the same File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|the same File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄04.jpg|the same File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|The stage of Amaterasumikado Shrine (天照御門神社) in Kasagi, where Kanjo Nawa is created. </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * [[Shimenawa]] * {{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り}} - Similar custom
== External links == * [http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452 Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)] - explanation by the local government of [[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]
[[:Category:Japanese folk religion]] {{culture-stub}}

'''Kanjo Nawa'''（{{lang-ja|勧請縄}}） is a custom of stretching a [[Shimenawa]] with [[Fetishism|fetishes]] hung at the border of a village. {{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り}}(道切り) is just a similar custom.
In rural area around Japan, there remains the custom of enshrining the items such as ropes of straw and grass, dolls, and straw sandals at the border of the village intended to prevent the evil such as an epidemic from entering the village, or to drive out the evil.<ref name="KOTOBANK">{{cite web| url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8B%A7%E8%AB%8B%E7%B8%84-1295507 | title=勧請縄(かんじょうなわ)とは - コトバンク | publisher={{ill|kotobank|ja|コトバンク}} | accessdate=2017-08-11 | language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|author=印南敏秀|editors= 植木行宣, 樋口昭|title=民俗文化分布圏論|date=1993-12-28|pages=375-376|isbn=4-626-01486-0}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=高嶋賢二|title=村境の大草履―四国西南部の事例を中心に|publisher=日本民具学会|journal=民具研究 |volume=131|year=2005|pages=45-61}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=小口千明|title=農村集落における精神的ムラ境の諸相 ―茨城県桜村における虫送りと道切りを事例として―|journal=城西人文研究|volume=12|pages=37-51|url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/els/contents110000187832.pdf?id=ART0000550707|date=1987-02-10}}</ref>
This custom can be found around [[Kinki Region]], especially in [[Wakasa]], [[Fukui Prefecture]], in the east and south of [[Shiga Prefecture]], in [[Iga, Mie|Iga, Mie Prefecture]], in the east mountainous region of [[Nara Prefecture]], and in Minami-Yamashiro of [[Kyoto Prefecture]]<ref>{{cite book|title=民俗文化分布圏論|author=印南敏秀|date=1993-12-28|isbn=4-626-01486-0|editors=植木行宣, 樋口昭|pages=377}}</ref>. Kanjo Nawa is also known as '''Kanjo Tsuri''' (勧請吊).<ref>{{cite journal|author=原田敏丸|year=1958|title=近江の勧請吊 彦根論叢|url=http://libdspace.biwako.shiga-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10441/7726/2/HIKONE%20RONSO_048-049_345-360Y%20harada.pdf|journal=人文科学特集第9号合併|volume=48/49|pages=345-360}}</ref>
== Examples == *Event of Kanjo Nawa in Asukaji (Kasukaji, [[Kasagi, Kyoto]]) *:In Asukaji, in every January, villagers create Shimenawa, which is stretched over Nunome-Gawa River, running through the village, in order to pray for prosperity and safety. One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as [[Cultural Property (Japan)|Search Results Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] in 1998.<ref>"飛鳥路の勧請縄行事" 京都の文化財第16集 March 1999 京都府教育委員会</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|accessdate=2017-09-11}}</ref>
<gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄01.jpg|Kanjo Nawa in Aasukaji File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄02.jpg|the same File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|the same File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄04.jpg|the same File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|The stage of Amaterasumikado Shrine (天照御門神社) in Kasagi, where Kanjo Nawa is created. </gallery>

[[:Category:Japanese folk religion]] {{culture-stub}}

revision.text:
'''Kanjo Nawa'''（{{lang-ja|勧請縄}}） is a custom of stretching a [[Shimenawa]] with [[Fetishism|fetishes]] hung at the border of a village. {{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り}}(道切り) is just a similar custom.
In rural area around Japan, there remains the custom of enshrining the items such as ropes of straw and grass, dolls, and straw sandals at the border of the village intended to prevent the evil such as an epidemic from entering the village, or to drive out the evil.<ref name="KOTOBANK">{{cite web| url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8B%A7%E8%AB%8B%E7%B8%84-1295507 | title=勧請縄(かんじょうなわ)とは - コトバンク | publisher={{ill|kotobank|ja|コトバンク}} | accessdate=2017-08-11 | language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|author=印南敏秀|editors= 植木行宣, 樋口昭|title=民俗文化分布圏論|date=1993-12-28|pages=375-376|isbn=4-626-01486-0}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=高嶋賢二|title=村境の大草履―四国西南部の事例を中心に|publisher=日本民具学会|journal=民具研究 |volume=131|year=2005|pages=45-61}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=小口千明|title=農村集落における精神的ムラ境の諸相 ―茨城県桜村における虫送りと道切りを事例として―|journal=城西人文研究|volume=12|pages=37-51|url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/els/contents110000187832.pdf?id=ART0000550707|date=1987-02-10}}</ref>
This custom can be found around [[Kinki Region]], especially in [[Wakasa]], [[Fukui Prefecture]], in the east and south of [[Shiga Prefecture]], in [[Iga, Mie|Iga, Mie Prefecture]], in the east mountainous region of [[Nara Prefecture]], and in Minami-Yamashiro of [[Kyoto Prefecture]]<ref>{{cite book|title=民俗文化分布圏論|author=印南敏秀|date=1993-12-28|isbn=4-626-01486-0|editors=植木行宣, 樋口昭|pages=377}}</ref>. It was recognized as [[Cultural Property (Japan)|Search Results Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] in 1998.<ref>"飛鳥路の勧請縄行事" 京都の文化財第16集 March 1999 京都府教育委員会</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|accessdate=2017-09-11}}</ref>

[[Category:Japanese folk religion]] {{Japan-culture-stub}}

'''Kanjo Nawa'''（{{lang-ja|勧請縄}}） is a Japanese custom of stretching a [[Shimenawa]] with [[Fetishism|fetishes]] hung at the border of a village. {{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り}}(道切り) is just a similar custom.
In rural area around Japan, there remains the custom of enshrining the items such as ropes of straw and grass, dolls, and straw sandals at the border of the village intended to prevent the evil such as an epidemic from entering the village, or to drive out the evil.<ref name="KOTOBANK">{{cite web| url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8B%A7%E8%AB%8B%E7%B8%84-1295507 | title=勧請縄(かんじょうなわ)とは - コトバンク | publisher={{ill|kotobank|ja|コトバンク}} | accessdate=2017-08-11 | language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|author=印南敏秀|editors= 植木行宣, 樋口昭|title=民俗文化分布圏論|date=1993-12-28|pages=375-376|isbn=4-626-01486-0}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=高嶋賢二|title=村境の大草履―四国西南部の事例を中心に|publisher=日本民具学会|journal=民具研究 |volume=131|year=2005|pages=45-61}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=小口千明|title=農村集落における精神的ムラ境の諸相 ―茨城県桜村における虫送りと道切りを事例として―|journal=城西人文研究|volume=12|pages=37-51|url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/els/contents110000187832.pdf?id=ART0000550707|date=1987-02-10}}</ref>
This custom can be found around [[Kinki Region]], especially in [[Wakasa]], [[Fukui Prefecture]], in the east and south of [[Shiga Prefecture]], in [[Iga, Mie|Iga, Mie Prefecture]], in the east mountainous region of [[Nara Prefecture]], and in Minami-Yamashiro of [[Kyoto Prefecture]]<ref>{{cite book|title=民俗文化分布圏論|author=印南敏秀|date=1993-12-28|isbn=4-626-01486-0|editors=植木行宣, 樋口昭|pages=377}}</ref>. Kanjo Nawa is also known as '''Kanjo Tsuri''' (勧請吊).<ref>{{cite journal|author=原田敏丸|year=1958|title=近江の勧請吊 彦根論叢|url=http://libdspace.biwako.shiga-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10441/7726/2/HIKONE%20RONSO_048-049_345-360Y%20harada.pdf|journal=人文科学特集第9号合併|volume=48/49|pages=345-360}}</ref>

One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as [[Cultural Property (Japan)|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] in 1998.<ref>"飛鳥路の勧請縄行事" 京都の文化財第16集 March 1999 京都府教育委員会</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|accessdate=2017-09-11}}</ref>

revision.comment: /* Examples */ remove some of the pictres that are duplicated
revision.text:
'''Kanjo Nawa'''（{{lang-ja|勧請縄}}） is a Japanese custom of stretching [[Shimenawa]], a variety of laid rope, with [[Fetishism|fetishes]] hung at the border of a village. {{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り}}(道切り) is just a similar custom. The term Kanjo Nawa also refers to the rope itself.
In rural area around Japan, there remains the custom of enshrining the items such as ropes of straw and grass, dolls, and straw sandals at the border of the village intended to prevent the evil such as an epidemic from entering the village, or to drive out the evil.<ref name="KOTOBANK">{{cite web| url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8B%A7%E8%AB%8B%E7%B8%84-1295507 | title=勧請縄(かんじょうなわ)とは - コトバンク | publisher={{ill|kotobank|ja|コトバンク}} | accessdate=2017-08-11 | language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|author=印南敏秀|editors= 植木行宣, 樋口昭|title=民俗文化分布圏論|date=1993-12-28|pages=375-376|isbn=4-626-01486-0| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=高嶋賢二|title=村境の大草履―四国西南部の事例を中心に|publisher=the Society for MINGU of Japan (日本民具学会)|journal=民具研究 |volume=131|year=2005|pages=45-61| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=小口千明|title=農村集落における精神的ムラ境の諸相 ―茨城県桜村における虫送りと道切りを事例として―|journal=城西人文研究|publisher=[[Josai University]]|volume=12|pages=37-51|url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/els/contents110000187832.pdf?id=ART0000550707|date=1987-02-10| language=ja}}</ref>
The custom called Kanjo Nawa can be found around [[Kinki Region]], especially in [[Wakasa, Fukui|Wakasa]], [[Fukui Prefecture]], in the east and south of [[Shiga Prefecture]], in [[Iga, Mie|Iga, Mie Prefecture]], in the east mountainous region of [[Nara Prefecture]], and in Minami-Yamashiro of [[Kyoto Prefecture]]<ref>{{cite book|title=民俗文化分布圏論|author=印南敏秀|date=1993-12-28|isbn=4-626-01486-0|editors=植木行宣, 樋口昭|pages=377| language=ja}}</ref>. Kanjo Nawa is also known as '''Kanjo Tsuri''' (勧請吊).<ref>{{cite journal|author=原田敏丸|year=1958|title=近江の勧請吊 彦根論叢|url=http://libdspace.biwako.shiga-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10441/7726/2/HIKONE%20RONSO_048-049_345-360Y%20harada.pdf|journal=人文科学特集第9号合併|publisher=Economic Society of [[Shiga University]] (滋賀大学経済学会)|volume=48/49|pages=345-360| language=ja}}</ref>
== Examples == *Kanjo Nawa in Asukaji, [[Kasagi, Kyoto]] *:In Asukaji, in every January, villagers create Shimenawa, which is stretched over Nunome-Gawa River, running through the village, in order to pray for prosperity and safety. One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as [[Cultural Property (Japan)|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] in 1998.<ref>{{cite book | title=京都の文化財第16集 | url=http://www.kyoto-be.ne.jp/bunkazai/cms/?action=common_download_main&upload_id=1280 | year=1999 | pages=33-34 | publisher=[[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefectural]] Board of Education| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|accessdate=2017-09-11}}</ref>
<gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|Kanjo Nawa stretched over the river in Asukaji File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|The stage of Amaterasumikado Shrine (天照御門神社) in Kasagi, where Kanjo Nawa is created. </gallery>

== External links == * [http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452 Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)] - explanation by the local government of [[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]
[[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Japanese religions terms]] {{Japan-culture-stub}}

In rural area around Japan, there remains the custom of enshrining the items such as ropes of straw and grass, dolls, and straw sandals at the border of the village intended to prevent the evil such as an epidemic from entering the village, or to drive out the evil.<ref name="KOTOBANK">{{cite web| url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8B%A7%E8%AB%8B%E7%B8%84-1295507 | title=勧請縄(かんじょうなわ)とは - コトバンク | publisher={{ill|kotobank|ja|コトバンク}} | accessdate=2017-08-11 | language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|author=印南敏秀|editors= 植木行宣, 樋口昭|title=民俗文化分布圏論|date=1993-12-28|pages=375-376|isbn=4-626-01486-0| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=高嶋賢二|title=村境の大草履―四国西南部の事例を中心に|publisher=the Society for MINGU of Japan (日本民具学会)|journal=民具研究 |volume=131|year=2005|pages=45-61| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=小口千明|title=農村集落における精神的ムラ境の諸相 ―茨城県桜村における虫送りと道切りを事例として―|journal=城西人文研究|publisher=[[Josai University]]|volume=12|pages=37-51|url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/els/contents110000187832.pdf?id=ART0000550707|date=1987-02-10| language=ja}}</ref>
The custom called Kanjo Nawa can be found around [[Kinki Region]], especially in [[Wakasa, Fukui|Wakasa]], [[Fukui Prefecture]], in the east and south of [[Shiga Prefecture]], in [[Iga, Mie|Iga, Mie Prefecture]], in the east mountainous region of [[Nara Prefecture]], and in Minami-Yamashiro of [[Kyoto Prefecture]]<ref>{{cite book|title=民俗文化分布圏論|author=印南敏秀|date=1993-12-28|isbn=4-626-01486-0|editors=植木行宣, 樋口昭|pages=377| language=ja}}</ref>. One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as [[Cultural Property (Japan)|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] in 1998.<ref>{{cite book | title=京都の文化財第16集 | url=http://www.kyoto-be.ne.jp/bunkazai/cms/?action=common_download_main&upload_id=1280 | year=1999 | pages=33-34 | publisher=[[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefectural]] Board of Education| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|accessdate=2017-09-11}}</ref>
<gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|Kanjo Nawa stretched over the river in Asukaji File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|The stage of Amaterasumikado Shrine (天照御門神社) in Kasagi, where Kanjo Nawa is created. </gallery>

'''Kanjo Nawa''' ({{lang-ja|勧請縄}}) is a Japanese custom of stretching [[Shimenawa]], a variety of laid rope, with [[Fetishism|fetishes]] hung at the border of a village. {{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り}}(道切り) is just a similar custom. The term Kanjo Nawa also refers to the rope itself.
In rural area around Japan, there remains the custom of enshrining the items such as ropes of straw and grass, dolls, and straw sandals at the border of the village intended to prevent the evil such as an epidemic from entering the village, or to drive out the evil.<ref name="KOTOBANK">{{cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8B%A7%E8%AB%8B%E7%B8%84-1295507 |script-title=ja:勧請縄(かんじょうなわ)とは - コトバンク |publisher=[[:ja:コトバンク|Kotobank]] |access-date=2017-08-11 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |author=印南敏秀 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |date=1993-12-28 |pages=375-376 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=高嶋賢二 |script-title=ja:村境の大草履―四国西南部の事例を中心に |publisher=The Society for MINGU of Japan (日本民具学会) |script-journal=ja:民具研究 |volume=131 |year=2005 |pages=45-61 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=小口千明 |script-title=ja:農村集落における精神的ムラ境の諸相 ―茨城県桜村における虫送りと道切りを事例として― |script-journal=ja:城西人文研究 |publisher=[[Josai University]] |volume=12 |pages=37–51 |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/els/contents110000187832.pdf?id=ART0000550707 |date=1987-02-10 |language=ja}}</ref>
The custom called Kanjo Nawa can be found around [[Kinki Region]], especially in [[Wakasa, Fukui|Wakasa]], [[Fukui Prefecture]], in the east and south of [[Shiga Prefecture]], in [[Iga, Mie|Iga, Mie Prefecture]], in the east mountainous region of [[Nara Prefecture]], and in Minami-Yamashiro of [[Kyoto Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite book |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |author=印南敏秀 |date=1993-12-28 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |page=377 |language=ja}}</ref> Kanjo Nawa is also known as '''Kanjo Tsuri''' (勧請吊).<ref>{{cite journal |author=原田敏丸 |year=1958 |script-title=ja:近江の勧請吊 彦根論叢 |url=http://libdspace.biwako.shiga-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10441/7726/2/HIKONE%20RONSO_048-049_345-360Y%20harada.pdf |script-journal=ja:人文科学特集第9号合併 |publisher=Economic Society of [[Shiga University]] (滋賀大学経済学会) |volume=48/49 |pages=345–360 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Examples == *Kanjo Nawa in Asukaji, [[Kasagi, Kyoto]] *:In Asukaji, in every January, villagers create Shimenawa, which is stretched over Nunome-Gawa River, running through the village, in order to pray for prosperity and safety. One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as [[Cultural Property (Japan)|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] in 1998.<ref>{{cite book | title=京都の文化財第16集 | url=http://www.kyoto-be.ne.jp/bunkazai/cms/?action=common_download_main&upload_id=1280 | year=1999 | pages=33-34 | publisher=[[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefectural]] Board of Education| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|accessdate=2017-09-11}}</ref>
<gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|Kanjo Nawa stretched over the river in Asukaji File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|The stage of Amaterasumikado Shrine (天照御門神社) in Kasagi, where Kanjo Nawa is created. </gallery>
== See also == * [[Shimenawa]] * {{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り}} - Similar custom
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External links == * [http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452 Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)] - explanation by the local government of [[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]
[[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Japanese religious terminology]] {{Japan-culture-stub}}

revision.text:
'''Kanjo Nawa''' ({{lang-ja|勧請縄}}) is a Japanese custom of stretching [[shimenawa]], a variety of laid rope, with [[Fetishism#Japan|fetishes]] hung at the border of a village. {{nihongo|{{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り}}|道切り}} is just a similar custom. The term Kanjo Nawa also refers to the rope itself.
In rural area around Japan, there remains the custom of enshrining the items such as ropes of straw and grass, dolls, and straw sandals at the border of the village intended to prevent the evil such as an epidemic from entering the village, or to drive out the evil.<ref name="KOTOBANK">{{cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8B%A7%E8%AB%8B%E7%B8%84-1295507 |script-title=ja:勧請縄(かんじょうなわ)とは – コトバンク |publisher=[[:ja:コトバンク|Kotobank]] |access-date=2017-08-11 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |author=印南敏秀 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |date=1993-12-28 |pages=375–376 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=高嶋賢二 |script-title=ja:村境の大草履―四国西南部の事例を中心に |publisher=The Society for MINGU of Japan (日本民具学会) |script-journal=ja:民具研究 |volume=131 |year=2005 |pages=45-61 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=小口千明 |script-title=ja:農村集落における精神的ムラ境の諸相 ―茨城県桜村における虫送りと道切りを事例として― |script-journal=ja:城西人文研究 |publisher=[[Josai University]] |volume=12 |pages=37–51 |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/els/contents110000187832.pdf?id=ART0000550707 |date=1987-02-10 |language=ja}}</ref>
The custom called Kanjo Nawa can be found around [[Kinki Region]], especially in [[Wakasa, Fukui|Wakasa]], [[Fukui Prefecture]], in the east and south of [[Shiga Prefecture]], in [[Iga, Mie|Iga, Mie Prefecture]], in the east mountainous region of [[Nara Prefecture]], and in Minami-Yamashiro of [[Kyoto Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite book |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |author=印南敏秀 |date=1993-12-28 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |page=377 |language=ja}}</ref> Kanjo Nawa is also known as '''Kanjo Tsuri''' (勧請吊).<ref>{{cite journal |author=原田敏丸 |year=1958 |script-title=ja:近江の勧請吊 彦根論叢 |url=http://libdspace.biwako.shiga-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10441/7726/2/HIKONE%20RONSO_048-049_345-360Y%20harada.pdf |script-journal=ja:人文科学特集第9号合併 |publisher=Economic Society of [[Shiga University]] (滋賀大学経済学会) |volume=48/49 |pages=345–360 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Examples == *Kanjo Nawa in Asukaji, [[Kasagi, Kyoto]] *:In Asukaji, in every January, villagers create Shimenawa, which is stretched over Nunome-Gawa River, running through the village, in order to pray for prosperity and safety. One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as [[Cultural Property (Japan)|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] in 1998.<ref>{{cite book | title=京都の文化財第16集 | url=http://www.kyoto-be.ne.jp/bunkazai/cms/?action=common_download_main&upload_id=1280 | year=1999 | pages=33–34 | publisher=[[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefectural]] Board of Education| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|access-date=2017-09-11}}</ref>
<gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|Kanjo Nawa stretched over the river in Asukaji File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|The stage of Amaterasumikado Shrine (天照御門神社) in Kasagi, where Kanjo Nawa is created. </gallery>
== See also == * {{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り}} – Similar custom * [[Shimenawa]]

[[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Japanese religious terminology]] {{Japan-culture-stub}}

'''Kanjo Nawa''' ({{lang-ja|勧請縄}}) is a Japanese custom of stretching [[shimenawa]], a variety of laid rope, with [[Fetishism#Japan|fetishes]] hung at the border of a village. {{nihongo|{{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り}}|道切り}} is just a similar custom. The term Kanjo Nawa also refers to the rope itself.
In rural area around Japan, there remains the custom of enshrining the items such as ropes of straw and grass, dolls, and straw sandals at the border of the village intended to prevent the evil such as an epidemic from entering the village, or to drive out the evil.<ref name="KOTOBANK">{{cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8B%A7%E8%AB%8B%E7%B8%84-1295507 |script-title=ja:勧請縄(かんじょうなわ)とは – コトバンク |publisher=[[:ja:コトバンク|Kotobank]] |access-date=2017-08-11 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |author=印南敏秀 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |date=1993-12-28 |pages=375–376 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=高嶋賢二 |script-title=ja:村境の大草履―四国西南部の事例を中心に |publisher=The Society for MINGU of Japan (日本民具学会) |script-journal=ja:民具研究 |volume=131 |year=2005 |pages=45-61 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=小口千明 |script-title=ja:農村集落における精神的ムラ境の諸相 ―茨城県桜村における虫送りと道切りを事例として― |script-journal=ja:城西人文研究 |publisher=[[Josai University]] |volume=12 |pages=37–51 |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/els/contents110000187832.pdf?id=ART0000550707 |date=1987-02-10 |language=ja}}</ref>
The custom called Kanjo Nawa can be found around [[Kinki Region]], especially in [[Wakasa, Fukui|Wakasa]], [[Fukui Prefecture]], in the east and south of [[Shiga Prefecture]], in [[Iga, Mie|Iga, Mie Prefecture]], in the east mountainous region of [[Nara Prefecture]], and in Minami-Yamashiro of [[Kyoto Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite book |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |author=印南敏秀 |date=1993-12-28 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |page=377 |language=ja}}</ref> Kanjo Nawa is also known as '''Kanjo Tsuri''' (勧請吊).<ref>{{cite journal |author=原田敏丸 |year=1958 |script-title=ja:近江の勧請吊 彦根論叢 |url=http://libdspace.biwako.shiga-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10441/7726/2/HIKONE%20RONSO_048-049_345-360Y%20harada.pdf |script-journal=ja:人文科学特集第9号合併 |publisher=Economic Society of [[Shiga University]] (滋賀大学経済学会) |volume=48/49 |pages=345–360 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Examples == *Kanjo Nawa in Asukaji, [[Kasagi, Kyoto]] *:In Asukaji, in every January, villagers create Shimenawa, which is stretched over Nunome-Gawa River, running through the village, in order to pray for prosperity and safety. One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as [[Cultural Property (Japan)|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] in 1998.<ref>{{cite book | title=京都の文化財第16集 | url=http://www.kyoto-be.ne.jp/bunkazai/cms/?action=common_download_main&upload_id=1280 | year=1999 | pages=33–34 | publisher=[[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefectural]] Board of Education| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|access-date=2017-09-11}}</ref>
<gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|Kanjo Nawa stretched over the river in Asukaji File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|The stage of Amaterasumikado Shrine (天照御門神社) in Kasagi, where Kanjo Nawa is created. </gallery>

[[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Japanese religious terminology]] [[Category:Test]] {{Japan-culture-stub}} {{Shinto shrine}}

'''Kanjo Nawa''' ({{lang-ja|勧請縄}}) is a Japanese custom of stretching [[shimenawa]], a variety of laid rope, with [[Fetishism#Japan|fetishes]] hung at the border of a village. {{nihongo|{{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り}}|道切り}} is just a similar custom. The term Kanjo Nawa also refers to the rope itself.
In rural area around Japan, there remains the custom of enshrining the items such as ropes of straw and grass, dolls, and straw sandals at the border of the village intended to prevent the evil such as an epidemic from entering the village, or to drive out the evil.<ref name="KOTOBANK">{{cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8B%A7%E8%AB%8B%E7%B8%84-1295507 |script-title=ja:勧請縄(かんじょうなわ)とは – コトバンク |publisher=[[:ja:コトバンク|Kotobank]] |access-date=2017-08-11 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |author=印南敏秀 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |date=1993-12-28 |pages=375–376 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=高嶋賢二 |script-title=ja:村境の大草履―四国西南部の事例を中心に |publisher=The Society for MINGU of Japan (日本民具学会) |script-journal=ja:民具研究 |volume=131 |year=2005 |pages=45-61 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=小口千明 |script-title=ja:農村集落における精神的ムラ境の諸相 ―茨城県桜村における虫送りと道切りを事例として― |script-journal=ja:城西人文研究 |publisher=[[Josai University]] |volume=12 |pages=37–51 |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/els/contents110000187832.pdf?id=ART0000550707 |date=1987-02-10 |language=ja}}</ref>
The custom called Kanjo Nawa can be found around [[Kinki Region]], especially in [[Wakasa, Fukui|Wakasa]], [[Fukui Prefecture]], in the east and south of [[Shiga Prefecture]], in [[Iga, Mie|Iga, Mie Prefecture]], in the east mountainous region of [[Nara Prefecture]], and in Minami-Yamashiro of [[Kyoto Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite book |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |author=印南敏秀 |date=1993-12-28 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |page=377 |language=ja}}</ref> Kanjo Nawa is also known as '''Kanjo Tsuri''' (勧請吊).<ref>{{cite journal |author=原田敏丸 |year=1958 |script-title=ja:近江の勧請吊 彦根論叢 |url=http://libdspace.biwako.shiga-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10441/7726/2/HIKONE%20RONSO_048-049_345-360Y%20harada.pdf |script-journal=ja:人文科学特集第9号合併 |publisher=Economic Society of [[Shiga University]] (滋賀大学経済学会) |volume=48/49 |pages=345–360 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Examples == *Kanjo Nawa in Asukaji, [[Kasagi, Kyoto]] *:In Asukaji, in every January, villagers create Shimenawa, which is stretched over Nunome-Gawa River, running through the village, in order to pray for prosperity and safety. One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as [[Cultural Property (Japan)|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] in 1998.<ref>{{cite book | title=京都の文化財第16集 | url=http://www.kyoto-be.ne.jp/bunkazai/cms/?action=common_download_main&upload_id=1280 | year=1999 | pages=33–34 | publisher=[[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefectural]] Board of Education| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|access-date=2017-09-11}}</ref>
<gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|Kanjo Nawa stretched over the river in Asukaji File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|The stage of Amaterasumikado Shrine (天照御門神社) in Kasagi, where Kanjo Nawa is created. </gallery>
== See also == * {{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り}} – Similar custom * [[Shimenawa]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External links == * [http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452 Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)] – explanation by the local government of Kasagi Town
[[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Japanese religious terminology]] {{Japan-culture-stub}} {{Shinto shrine}}

The custom called Kanjo Nawa can be found around [[Kinki Region]], especially in [[Wakasa, Fukui|Wakasa]], [[Fukui Prefecture]], in the east and south of [[Shiga Prefecture]], in [[Iga, Mie|Iga, Mie Prefecture]], in the east mountainous region of [[Nara Prefecture]], and in Minami-Yamashiro of [[Kyoto Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite book |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |author=印南敏秀 |date=1993-12-28 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |page=377 |language=ja}}</ref> Kanjo Nawa is also known as '''Kanjo Tsuri''' (勧請吊).<ref>{{cite journal |author=原田敏丸 |year=1958 |script-title=ja:近江の勧請吊 彦根論叢 |url=http://libdspace.biwako.shiga-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10441/7726/2/HIKONE%20RONSO_048-049_345-360Y%20harada.pdf |script-journal=ja:人文科学特集第9号合併 |publisher=Economic Society of [[Shiga University]] (滋賀大学経済学会) |volume=48/49 |pages=345–360 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Examples == *Kanjo Nawa in Asukaji, [[Kasagi, Kyoto]] *:In Asukaji, in every January, villagers create Shimenawa, which is stretched over Nunome-Gawa River, running through the village, in order to pray for prosperity and safety. One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as [[Cultural Property (Japan)|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] in 1998.<ref>{{cite book | title=京都の文化財第16集 | url=http://www.kyoto-be.ne.jp/bunkazai/cms/?action=common_download_main&upload_id=1280 | year=1999 | pages=33–34 | publisher=[[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefectural]] Board of Education| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|access-date=2017-09-11}}</ref>
<gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|Kanjo Nawa stretched over the river in Asukaji File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|The stage of Amaterasumikado Shrine (天照御門神社) in Kasagi, where Kanjo Nawa is created. </gallery>

[[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Japanese religious terminology]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia1]] [[en:Kanjo Nawa]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia1]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia1]] [[en:Kanjo Nawa]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia1]] {{Japan-culture-stub}} {{Shinto shrine}}

revision.comment: Moving from [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia1]] to [[Category:Top level categories]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Kanjo Nawa''' ({{lang-ja|勧請縄}}) is a Japanese custom of stretching [[shimenawa]], a variety of laid rope, with [[Fetishism#Japan|fetishes]] hung at the border of a village. {{nihongo|{{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り}}|道切り}} is just a similar custom. The term Kanjo Nawa also refers to the rope itself.
In rural area around Japan, there remains the custom of enshrining the items such as ropes of straw and grass, dolls, and straw sandals at the border of the village intended to prevent the evil such as an epidemic from entering the village, or to drive out the evil.<ref name="KOTOBANK">{{cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8B%A7%E8%AB%8B%E7%B8%84-1295507 |script-title=ja:勧請縄(かんじょうなわ)とは – コトバンク |publisher=[[:ja:コトバンク|Kotobank]] |access-date=2017-08-11 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |author=印南敏秀 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |date=1993-12-28 |pages=375–376 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=高嶋賢二 |script-title=ja:村境の大草履―四国西南部の事例を中心に |publisher=The Society for MINGU of Japan (日本民具学会) |script-journal=ja:民具研究 |volume=131 |year=2005 |pages=45-61 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=小口千明 |script-title=ja:農村集落における精神的ムラ境の諸相 ―茨城県桜村における虫送りと道切りを事例として― |script-journal=ja:城西人文研究 |publisher=[[Josai University]] |volume=12 |pages=37–51 |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/els/contents110000187832.pdf?id=ART0000550707 |date=1987-02-10 |language=ja}}</ref>
The custom called Kanjo Nawa can be found around [[Kinki Region]], especially in [[Wakasa, Fukui|Wakasa]], [[Fukui Prefecture]], in the east and south of [[Shiga Prefecture]], in [[Iga, Mie|Iga, Mie Prefecture]], in the east mountainous region of [[Nara Prefecture]], and in Minami-Yamashiro of [[Kyoto Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite book |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |author=印南敏秀 |date=1993-12-28 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |page=377 |language=ja}}</ref> Kanjo Nawa is also known as '''Kanjo Tsuri''' (勧請吊).<ref>{{cite journal |author=原田敏丸 |year=1958 |script-title=ja:近江の勧請吊 彦根論叢 |url=http://libdspace.biwako.shiga-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10441/7726/2/HIKONE%20RONSO_048-049_345-360Y%20harada.pdf |script-journal=ja:人文科学特集第9号合併 |publisher=Economic Society of [[Shiga University]] (滋賀大学経済学会) |volume=48/49 |pages=345–360 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Examples == *Kanjo Nawa in Asukaji, [[Kasagi, Kyoto]] *:In Asukaji, in every January, villagers create Shimenawa, which is stretched over Nunome-Gawa River, running through the village, in order to pray for prosperity and safety. One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as [[Cultural Property (Japan)|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] in 1998.<ref>{{cite book | title=京都の文化財第16集 | url=http://www.kyoto-be.ne.jp/bunkazai/cms/?action=common_download_main&upload_id=1280 | year=1999 | pages=33–34 | publisher=[[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefectural]] Board of Education| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|access-date=2017-09-11}}</ref>
<gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|Kanjo Nawa stretched over the river in Asukaji File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|The stage of Amaterasumikado Shrine (天照御門神社) in Kasagi, where Kanjo Nawa is created. </gallery>
== See also == * {{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り}} – Similar custom * [[Shimenawa]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External links == * [http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452 Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)] – explanation by the local government of Kasagi Town

'''Kanjo Nawa''' ({{lang-ja|勧請縄}}) is a Japanese custom of stretching [[shimenawa]], a variety of laid rope, with [[Fetishism#Japan|fetishes]] hung at the border of a village. {{nihongo|{{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り}}|道切り}} is just a similar custom. The term Kanjo Nawa also refers to the rope itself.
In rural area around Japan, there remains the custom of enshrining the items such as ropes of straw and grass, dolls, and straw sandals at the border of the village intended to prevent the evil such as an epidemic from entering the village, or to drive out the evil.<ref name="KOTOBANK">{{cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8B%A7%E8%AB%8B%E7%B8%84-1295507 |script-title=ja:勧請縄(かんじょうなわ)とは – コトバンク |publisher=[[:ja:コトバンク|Kotobank]] |access-date=2017-08-11 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |author=印南敏秀 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |date=1993-12-28 |pages=375–376 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=高嶋賢二 |script-title=ja:村境の大草履―四国西南部の事例を中心に |publisher=The Society for MINGU of Japan (日本民具学会) |script-journal=ja:民具研究 |volume=131 |year=2005 |pages=45-61 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=小口千明 |script-title=ja:農村集落における精神的ムラ境の諸相 ―茨城県桜村における虫送りと道切りを事例として― |script-journal=ja:城西人文研究 |publisher=[[Josai University]] |volume=12 |pages=37–51 |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/els/contents110000187832.pdf?id=ART0000550707 |date=1987-02-10 |language=ja}}</ref>
The custom called Kanjo Nawa can be found around [[Kinki Region]], especially in [[Wakasa, Fukui|Wakasa]], [[Fukui Prefecture]], in the east and south of [[Shiga Prefecture]], in [[Iga, Mie|Iga, Mie Prefecture]], in the east mountainous region of [[Nara Prefecture]], and in Minami-Yamashiro of [[Kyoto Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite book |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |author=印南敏秀 |date=1993-12-28 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |page=377 |language=ja}}</ref> Kanjo Nawa is also known as '''Kanjo Tsuri''' (勧請吊).<ref>{{cite journal |author=原田敏丸 |year=1958 |script-title=ja:近江の勧請吊 彦根論叢 |url=http://libdspace.biwako.shiga-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10441/7726/2/HIKONE%20RONSO_048-049_345-360Y%20harada.pdf |script-journal=ja:人文科学特集第9号合併 |publisher=Economic Society of [[Shiga University]] (滋賀大学経済学会) |volume=48/49 |pages=345–360 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Examples == *Kanjo Nawa in Asukaji, [[Kasagi, Kyoto]] *:In Asukaji, in every January, villagers create Shimenawa, which is stretched over Nunome-Gawa River, running through the village, in order to pray for prosperity and safety. One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as [[Cultural Property (Japan)|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] in 1998.<ref>{{cite book | title=京都の文化財第16集 | url=http://www.kyoto-be.ne.jp/bunkazai/cms/?action=common_download_main&upload_id=1280 | year=1999 | pages=33–34 | publisher=[[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefectural]] Board of Education| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|access-date=2017-09-11}}</ref>
<gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|Kanjo Nawa stretched over the river in Asukaji File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|The stage of Amaterasumikado Shrine (天照御門神社) in Kasagi, where Kanjo Nawa is created. </gallery>

== External links == * [http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452 Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)] – explanation by the local government of Kasagi Town
[[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Japanese religious terminology]] [[en:Kanjo Nawa]][[en:Kanjo Nawa]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia39]] [[Category:dd]] {{Japan-culture-stub}} {{Shinto shrine}}

'''Kanjo Nawa''' ({{lang-ja|勧請縄}}) is a Japanese custom of stretching [[shimenawa]], a variety of laid rope, with [[Fetishism#Japan|fetishes]] hung at the border of a village. {{nihongo|{{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り}}|道切り}} is just a similar custom. The term Kanjo Nawa also refers to the rope itself.
In rural area around Japan, there remains the custom of enshrining the items such as ropes of straw and grass, dolls, and straw sandals at the border of the village intended to prevent the evil such as an epidemic from entering the village, or to drive out the evil.<ref name="KOTOBANK">{{cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8B%A7%E8%AB%8B%E7%B8%84-1295507 |script-title=ja:勧請縄(かんじょうなわ)とは – コトバンク |publisher=[[:ja:コトバンク|Kotobank]] |access-date=2017-08-11 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |author=印南敏秀 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |date=1993-12-28 |pages=375–376 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=高嶋賢二 |script-title=ja:村境の大草履―四国西南部の事例を中心に |publisher=The Society for MINGU of Japan (日本民具学会) |script-journal=ja:民具研究 |volume=131 |year=2005 |pages=45-61 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=小口千明 |script-title=ja:農村集落における精神的ムラ境の諸相 ―茨城県桜村における虫送りと道切りを事例として― |script-journal=ja:城西人文研究 |publisher=[[Josai University]] |volume=12 |pages=37–51 |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/els/contents110000187832.pdf?id=ART0000550707 |date=1987-02-10 |language=ja}}</ref>
The custom called Kanjo Nawa can be found around [[Kinki Region]], especially in [[Wakasa, Fukui|Wakasa]], [[Fukui Prefecture]], in the east and south of [[Shiga Prefecture]], in [[Iga, Mie|Iga, Mie Prefecture]], in the east mountainous region of [[Nara Prefecture]], and in Minami-Yamashiro of [[Kyoto Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite book |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |author=印南敏秀 |date=1993-12-28 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |page=377 |language=ja}}</ref> Kanjo Nawa is also known as '''Kanjo Tsuri''' (勧請吊).<ref>{{cite journal |author=原田敏丸 |year=1958 |script-title=ja:近江の勧請吊 彦根論叢 |url=http://libdspace.biwako.shiga-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10441/7726/2/HIKONE%20RONSO_048-049_345-360Y%20harada.pdf |script-journal=ja:人文科学特集第9号合併 |publisher=Economic Society of [[Shiga University]] (滋賀大学経済学会) |volume=48/49 |pages=345–360 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Examples == *Kanjo Nawa in Asukaji, [[Kasagi, Kyoto]] *:In Asukaji, in every January, villagers create Shimenawa, which is stretched over Nunome-Gawa River, running through the village, in order to pray for prosperity and safety. One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as [[Cultural Property (Japan)|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] in 1998.<ref>{{cite book | title=京都の文化財第16集 | url=http://www.kyoto-be.ne.jp/bunkazai/cms/?action=common_download_main&upload_id=1280 | year=1999 | pages=33–34 | publisher=[[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefectural]] Board of Education| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|access-date=2017-09-11}}</ref>
<gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|Kanjo Nawa stretched over the river in Asukaji File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|The stage of Amaterasumikado Shrine (天照御門神社) in Kasagi, where Kanjo Nawa is created. </gallery>
== See also == * {{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り}} – Similar custom * [[Shimenawa]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External links == * [http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452 Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)] – explanation by the local government of Kasagi Town

In rural area around Japan, there remains the custom of enshrining the items such as ropes of straw and grass, dolls, and straw sandals at the border of the village intended to prevent the evil such as an epidemic from entering the village, or to drive out the evil.<ref name="KOTOBANK">{{cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8B%A7%E8%AB%8B%E7%B8%84-1295507 |script-title=ja:勧請縄(かんじょうなわ)とは – コトバンク |publisher=[[:ja:コトバンク|Kotobank]] |access-date=2017-08-11 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |author=印南敏秀 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |date=1993-12-28 |pages=375–376 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=高嶋賢二 |script-title=ja:村境の大草履―四国西南部の事例を中心に |publisher=The Society for MINGU of Japan (日本民具学会) |script-journal=ja:民具研究 |volume=131 |year=2005 |pages=45-61 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=小口千明 |script-title=ja:農村集落における精神的ムラ境の諸相 ―茨城県桜村における虫送りと道切りを事例として― |script-journal=ja:城西人文研究 |publisher=[[Josai University]] |volume=12 |pages=37–51 |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/els/contents110000187832.pdf?id=ART0000550707 |date=1987-02-10 |language=ja}}</ref>
The custom called Kanjo Nawa can be found around [[Kinki Region]], especially in [[Wakasa, Fukui|Wakasa]], [[Fukui Prefecture]], in the east and south of [[Shiga Prefecture]], in [[Iga, Mie|Iga, Mie Prefecture]], in the east mountainous region of [[Nara Prefecture]], and in Minami-Yamashiro of [[Kyoto Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite book |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |author=印南敏秀 |date=1993-12-28 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |page=377 |language=ja}}</ref> Kanjo Nawa is also known as '''Kanjo Tsuri''' (勧請吊).<ref>{{cite journal |author=原田敏丸 |year=1958 |script-title=ja:近江の勧請吊 彦根論叢 |url=http://libdspace.biwako.shiga-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10441/7726/2/HIKONE%20RONSO_048-049_345-360Y%20harada.pdf |script-journal=ja:人文科学特集第9号合併 |publisher=Economic Society of [[Shiga University]] (滋賀大学経済学会) |volume=48/49 |pages=345–360 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Examples == *Kanjo Nawa in Asukaji, [[Kasagi, Kyoto]] *:In Asukaji, in every January, villagers create Shimenawa, which is stretched over Nunome-Gawa River, running through the village, in order to pray for prosperity and safety. One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as [[Cultural Property (Japan)|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] in 1998.<ref>{{cite book | title=京都の文化財第16集 | url=http://www.kyoto-be.ne.jp/bunkazai/cms/?action=common_download_main&upload_id=1280 | year=1999 | pages=33–34 | publisher=[[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefectural]] Board of Education| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|access-date=2017-09-11}}</ref>
<gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|Kanjo Nawa stretched over the river in Asukaji File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|The stage of Amaterasumikado Shrine (天照御門神社) in Kasagi, where Kanjo Nawa is created. </gallery>

'''Kanjo Nawa''' ({{lang-ja|勧請縄}}) is a Japanese custom of stretching [[shimenawa]], a variety of laid rope, with [[Fetishism#Japan|fetishes]] hung at the border of a village. {{nihongo|{{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り}}|道切り}} is just a similar custom. The term Kanjo Nawa also refers to the rope itself.
In rural area around Japan, there remains the custom of enshrining the items such as ropes of straw and grass, dolls, and straw sandals at the border of the village intended to prevent the evil such as an epidemic from entering the village, or to drive out the evil.<ref name="KOTOBANK">{{cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8B%A7%E8%AB%8B%E7%B8%84-1295507 |script-title=ja:勧請縄(かんじょうなわ)とは – コトバンク |publisher=[[:ja:コトバンク|Kotobank]] |access-date=2017-08-11 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |author=印南敏秀 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |date=1993-12-28 |pages=375–376 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=高嶋賢二 |script-title=ja:村境の大草履―四国西南部の事例を中心に |publisher=The Society for MINGU of Japan (日本民具学会) |script-journal=ja:民具研究 |volume=131 |year=2005 |pages=45-61 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=小口千明 |script-title=ja:農村集落における精神的ムラ境の諸相 ―茨城県桜村における虫送りと道切りを事例として― |script-journal=ja:城西人文研究 |publisher=[[Josai University]] |volume=12 |pages=37–51 |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/els/contents110000187832.pdf?id=ART0000550707 |date=1987-02-10 |language=ja}}</ref>
The custom called Kanjo Nawa can be found around [[Kinki Region]], especially in [[Wakasa, Fukui|Wakasa]], [[Fukui Prefecture]], in the east and south of [[Shiga Prefecture]], in [[Iga, Mie|Iga, Mie Prefecture]], in the east mountainous region of [[Nara Prefecture]], and in Minami-Yamashiro of [[Kyoto Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite book |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |author=印南敏秀 |date=1993-12-28 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |page=377 |language=ja}}</ref> Kanjo Nawa is also known as '''Kanjo Tsuri''' (勧請吊).<ref>{{cite journal |author=原田敏丸 |year=1958 |script-title=ja:近江の勧請吊 彦根論叢 |url=http://libdspace.biwako.shiga-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10441/7726/2/HIKONE%20RONSO_048-049_345-360Y%20harada.pdf |script-journal=ja:人文科学特集第9号合併 |publisher=Economic Society of [[Shiga University]] (滋賀大学経済学会) |volume=48/49 |pages=345–360 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Examples == *Kanjo Nawa in Asukaji, [[Kasagi, Kyoto]] *:In Asukaji, in every January, villagers create Shimenawa, which is stretched over Nunome-Gawa River, running through the village, in order to pray for prosperity and safety. One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as [[Cultural Property (Japan)|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] in 1998.<ref>{{cite book | title=京都の文化財第16集 | url=http://www.kyoto-be.ne.jp/bunkazai/cms/?action=common_download_main&upload_id=1280 | year=1999 | pages=33–34 | publisher=[[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefectural]] Board of Education| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|access-date=2017-09-11}}</ref>
<gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|Kanjo Nawa stretched over the river in Asukaji File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|The stage of Amaterasumikado Shrine (天照御門神社) in Kasagi, where Kanjo Nawa is created. </gallery>
== See also == * {{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り}} – Similar custom * [[Shimenawa]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External links == * [http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452 Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)] – explanation by the local government of Kasagi Town
[[category:Japanese folk religion]] [[category:Japanese religious terminology]] [[en:Kanjo Nawa]][[en:Kanjo Nawa]][[category:Pages and categories from wikipedia]] {{Japan-culture-stub}} {{Shinto shrine}}

The custom called Kanjo Nawa can be found around [[Kinki Region]], especially in [[Wakasa, Fukui|Wakasa]], [[Fukui Prefecture]], in the east and south of [[Shiga Prefecture]], in [[Iga, Mie|Iga, Mie Prefecture]], in the east mountainous region of [[Nara Prefecture]], and in Minami-Yamashiro of [[Kyoto Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite book |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |author=印南敏秀 |date=1993-12-28 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |page=377 |language=ja}}</ref> Kanjo Nawa is also known as '''Kanjo Tsuri''' (勧請吊).<ref>{{cite journal |author=原田敏丸 |year=1958 |script-title=ja:近江の勧請吊 彦根論叢 |url=http://libdspace.biwako.shiga-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10441/7726/2/HIKONE%20RONSO_048-049_345-360Y%20harada.pdf |script-journal=ja:人文科学特集第9号合併 |publisher=Economic Society of [[Shiga University]] (滋賀大学経済学会) |volume=48/49 |pages=345–360 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Examples == *Kanjo Nawa in Asukaji, [[Kasagi, Kyoto]] *:In Asukaji, in every January, villagers create Shimenawa, which is stretched over Nunome-Gawa River, running through the village, in order to pray for prosperity and safety. One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as [[Cultural Property (Japan)|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] in 1998.<ref>{{cite book | title=京都の文化財第16集 | url=http://www.kyoto-be.ne.jp/bunkazai/cms/?action=common_download_main&upload_id=1280 | year=1999 | pages=33–34 | publisher=[[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefectural]] Board of Education| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|access-date=2017-09-11}}</ref>
<gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|Kanjo Nawa stretched over the river in Asukaji File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|The stage of Amaterasumikado Shrine (天照御門神社) in Kasagi, where Kanjo Nawa is created. </gallery>

'''Kanjo Nawa''' ({{lang-ja|勧請縄}}) is a Japanese custom of stretching [[shimenawa]], a variety of laid rope, with [[Fetishism#Japan|fetishes]] hung at the border of a village. {{nihongo|{{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り}}|道切り}} is just a similar custom. The term Kanjo Nawa also refers to the rope itself.
In rural area around Japan, there remains the custom of enshrining the items such as ropes of straw and grass, dolls, and straw sandals at the border of the village intended to prevent the evil such as an epidemic from entering the village, or to drive out the evil.<ref name="KOTOBANK">{{cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8B%A7%E8%AB%8B%E7%B8%84-1295507 |script-title=ja:勧請縄(かんじょうなわ)とは – コトバンク |publisher=[[:ja:コトバンク|Kotobank]] |access-date=2017-08-11 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |author=印南敏秀 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |date=1993-12-28 |pages=375–376 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=高嶋賢二 |script-title=ja:村境の大草履―四国西南部の事例を中心に |publisher=The Society for MINGU of Japan (日本民具学会) |script-journal=ja:民具研究 |volume=131 |year=2005 |pages=45-61 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=小口千明 |script-title=ja:農村集落における精神的ムラ境の諸相 ―茨城県桜村における虫送りと道切りを事例として― |script-journal=ja:城西人文研究 |publisher=[[Josai University]] |volume=12 |pages=37–51 |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/els/contents110000187832.pdf?id=ART0000550707 |date=1987-02-10 |language=ja}}</ref>
The custom called Kanjo Nawa can be found around [[Kinki Region]], especially in [[Wakasa, Fukui|Wakasa]], [[Fukui Prefecture]], in the east and south of [[Shiga Prefecture]], in [[Iga, Mie|Iga, Mie Prefecture]], in the east mountainous region of [[Nara Prefecture]], and in Minami-Yamashiro of [[Kyoto Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite book |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |author=印南敏秀 |date=1993-12-28 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |page=377 |language=ja}}</ref> Kanjo Nawa is also known as '''Kanjo Tsuri''' (勧請吊).<ref>{{cite journal |author=原田敏丸 |year=1958 |script-title=ja:近江の勧請吊 彦根論叢 |url=http://libdspace.biwako.shiga-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10441/7726/2/HIKONE%20RONSO_048-049_345-360Y%20harada.pdf |script-journal=ja:人文科学特集第9号合併 |publisher=Economic Society of [[Shiga University]] (滋賀大学経済学会) |volume=48/49 |pages=345–360 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Examples == *Kanjo Nawa in Asukaji, [[Kasagi, Kyoto]] *:In Asukaji, in every January, villagers create Shimenawa, which is stretched over Nunome-Gawa River, running through the village, in order to pray for prosperity and safety. One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as [[Cultural Property (Japan)|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] in 1998.<ref>{{cite book | title=京都の文化財第16集 | url=http://www.kyoto-be.ne.jp/bunkazai/cms/?action=common_download_main&upload_id=1280 | year=1999 | pages=33–34 | publisher=[[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefectural]] Board of Education| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|access-date=2017-09-11}}</ref>
<gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|Kanjo Nawa stretched over the river in Asukaji File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|The stage of Amaterasumikado Shrine (天照御門神社) in Kasagi, where Kanjo Nawa is created. </gallery>
== See also == * {{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り}} – Similar custom * [[Shimenawa]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External links == * [http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452 Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)] – explanation by the local government of Kasagi Town

== Examples == *Kanjo Nawa in Asukaji, [[Kasagi, Kyoto]] *:In Asukaji, in every January, villagers create Shimenawa, which is stretched over Nunome-Gawa River, running through the village, in order to pray for prosperity and safety. One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. <gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|Kanjo Nawa stretched over the river in Asukaji File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|The stage of Amaterasumikado Shrine (天照御門神社) in Kasagi, where Kanjo Nawa is created. </gallery>

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Kanjo Nawa''' ({{lang-ja|勧請縄}}) is a Japanese custom of stretching [[shimenawa]], a variety of laid rope, with [[Fetishism#Japan|fetishes]] hung at the border of a village. {{nihongo|{{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り}}|道切り}} is just a similar custom. The term Kanjo Nawa also refers to the rope itself.
In rural area around Japan, there remains the custom of enshrining the items such as ropes of straw and grass, dolls, and straw sandals at the border of the village intended to prevent the evil such as an epidemic from entering the village, or to drive out the evil.<ref name="KOTOBANK">{{cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8B%A7%E8%AB%8B%E7%B8%84-1295507 |script-title=ja:勧請縄(かんじょうなわ)とは – コトバンク |publisher=[[:ja:コトバンク|Kotobank]] |access-date=2017-08-11 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |author=印南敏秀 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |date=1993-12-28 |pages=375–376 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=高嶋賢二 |script-title=ja:村境の大草履―四国西南部の事例を中心に |publisher=The Society for MINGU of Japan (日本民具学会) |script-journal=ja:民具研究 |volume=131 |year=2005 |pages=45-61 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=小口千明 |script-title=ja:農村集落における精神的ムラ境の諸相 ―茨城県桜村における虫送りと道切りを事例として― |script-journal=ja:城西人文研究 |publisher=[[Josai University]] |volume=12 |pages=37–51 |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/els/contents110000187832.pdf?id=ART0000550707 |date=1987-02-10 |language=ja}}</ref>
The custom called Kanjo Nawa can be found around [[Kinki Region]], especially in [[Wakasa, Fukui|Wakasa]], [[Fukui Prefecture]], in the east and south of [[Shiga Prefecture]], in [[Iga, Mie|Iga, Mie Prefecture]], in the east mountainous region of [[Nara Prefecture]], and in Minami-Yamashiro of [[Kyoto Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite book |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |author=印南敏秀 |date=1993-12-28 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |page=377 |language=ja}}</ref> Kanjo Nawa is also known as '''Kanjo Tsuri''' (勧請吊).<ref>{{cite journal |author=原田敏丸 |year=1958 |script-title=ja:近江の勧請吊 彦根論叢 |url=http://libdspace.biwako.shiga-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10441/7726/2/HIKONE%20RONSO_048-049_345-360Y%20harada.pdf |script-journal=ja:人文科学特集第9号合併 |publisher=Economic Society of [[Shiga University]] (滋賀大学経済学会) |volume=48/49 |pages=345–360 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Examples == *Kanjo Nawa in Asukaji, [[Kasagi, Kyoto]] *:In Asukaji, in every January, villagers create Shimenawa, which is stretched over Nunome-Gawa River, running through the village, in order to pray for prosperity and safety. One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as [[Cultural Property (Japan)|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] in 1998.<ref>{{cite book | title=京都の文化財第16集 | url=http://www.kyoto-be.ne.jp/bunkazai/cms/?action=common_download_main&upload_id=1280 | year=1999 | pages=33–34 | publisher=[[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefectural]] Board of Education| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|access-date=2017-09-11}}</ref>
<gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|Kanjo Nawa stretched over the river in Asukaji File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|The stage of Amaterasumikado Shrine (天照御門神社) in Kasagi, where Kanjo Nawa is created. </gallery>
== See also == * {{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り}} – Similar custom * [[Shimenawa]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External links == * [http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452 Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)] – explanation by the local government of Kasagi Town

== Examples == *Kanjo Nawa in Asukaji, [[Kasagi, Kyoto]] *:In Asukaji, in every January, villagers create Shimenawa, which is stretched over Nunome-Gawa River, running through the village, in order to pray for prosperity and safety. One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as [[Cultural Property (Japan)|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] in 1998.<ref>{{cite book | title=京都の文化財第16集 | url=http://www.kyoto-be.ne.jp/bunkazai/cms/?action=common_download_main&upload_id=1280 | year=1999 | pages=33–34 | publisher=[[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefectural]] Board of Education| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|access-date=2017-09-11}}</ref>
<gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|Kanjo Nawa stretched over the river in Asukaji File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|The stage of Amaterasumikado Shrine (天照御門神社) in Kasagi, where Kanjo Nawa is created. </gallery>

One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as [[Cultural Property (Japan)|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] in 1998.<ref>{{cite book | title=京都の文化財第16集 | url=http://www.kyoto-be.ne.jp/bunkazai/cms/?action=common_download_main&upload_id=1280 | year=1999 | pages=33–34 | publisher=[[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefectural]] Board of Education| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|access-date=2017-09-11}}</ref>

'''Kanjo Nawa''' ({{lang-ja|勧請縄}}) is a Japanese custom of stretching [[shimenawa]], a variety of laid rope, with [[Fetishism#Japan|fetishes]] hung at the border of a village. {{nihongo|{{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り}}|道切り}} is just a similar custom. The term Kanjo Nawa also refers to the rope itself.

'''Kanjo Nawa''' ({{lang-ja|勧請縄}}) is a Japanese custom of stretching [[shimenawa]], a variety of laid rope, with [[Fetishism#Japan|fetishes]] hung at the border of a village. {{nihongo|{{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り}}|道切り}} is just a similar custom. The term Kanjo Nawa also refers to the rope itself.
In rural area around Japan, there remains the custom of enshrining the items such as ropes of straw and grass, dolls, and straw sandals at the border of the village intended to prevent the evil such as an epidemic from entering the village, or to drive out the evil.<ref name="KOTOBANK">{{cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8B%A7%E8%AB%8B%E7%B8%84-1295507 |script-title=ja:勧請縄(かんじょうなわ)とは – コトバンク |publisher=[[:ja:コトバンク|Kotobank]] |access-date=2017-08-11 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |author=印南敏秀 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |date=1993-12-28 |pages=375–376 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=高嶋賢二 |script-title=ja:村境の大草履―四国西南部の事例を中心に |publisher=The Society for MINGU of Japan (日本民具学会) |script-journal=ja:民具研究 |volume=131 |year=2005 |pages=45-61 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=小口千明 |script-title=ja:農村集落における精神的ムラ境の諸相 ―茨城県桜村における虫送りと道切りを事例として― |script-journal=ja:城西人文研究 |publisher=[[Josai University]] |volume=12 |pages=37–51 |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/els/contents110000187832.pdf?id=ART0000550707 |date=1987-02-10 |language=ja}}</ref>
The custom called Kanjo Nawa can be found around [[Kinki Region]], especially in [[Wakasa, Fukui|Wakasa]], [[Fukui Prefecture]], in the east and south of [[Shiga Prefecture]], in [[Iga, Mie|Iga, Mie Prefecture]], in the east mountainous region of [[Nara Prefecture]], and in Minami-Yamashiro of [[Kyoto Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite book |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |author=印南敏秀 |date=1993-12-28 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |page=377 |language=ja}}</ref> Kanjo Nawa is also known as '''Kanjo Tsuri''' (勧請吊).<ref>{{cite journal |author=原田敏丸 |year=1958 |script-title=ja:近江の勧請吊 彦根論叢 |url=http://libdspace.biwako.shiga-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10441/7726/2/HIKONE%20RONSO_048-049_345-360Y%20harada.pdf |script-journal=ja:人文科学特集第9号合併 |publisher=Economic Society of [[Shiga University]] (滋賀大学経済学会) |volume=48/49 |pages=345–360 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Examples == *Kanjo Nawa in Asukaji, [[Kasagi, Kyoto]] *:In Asukaji, in every January, villagers create Shimenawa, which is stretched over Nunome-Gawa River, running through the village, in order to pray for prosperity and safety. One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as [[Cultural Property (Japan)|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] in 1998.<ref>{{cite book | title=京都の文化財第16集 | url=http://www.kyoto-be.ne.jp/bunkazai/cms/?action=common_download_main&upload_id=1280 | year=1999 | pages=33–34 | publisher=[[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefectural]] Board of Education| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|access-date=2017-09-11}}</ref>
<gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|Kanjo Nawa stretched over the river in Asukaji File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|The stage of Amaterasumikado Shrine (天照御門神社) in Kasagi, where Kanjo Nawa is created. </gallery>

'''Kanjo Nawa''' ({{lang-ja|勧請縄}}) is a Japanese custom of stretching [[shimenawa]], a variety of laid rope, with [[Fetishism#Japan|fetishes]] hung at the border of a village. {{nihongo|{{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り}}|道切り}} is just a similar custom. The term Kanjo Nawa also refers to the rope itself.
In rural area around Japan, there remains the custom of enshrining the items such as ropes of straw and grass, dolls, and straw sandals at the border of the village intended to prevent the evil such as an epidemic from entering the village, or to drive out the evil.<ref name="KOTOBANK">{{cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8B%A7%E8%AB%8B%E7%B8%84-1295507 |script-title=ja:勧請縄(かんじょうなわ)とは – コトバンク |publisher=[[:ja:コトバンク|Kotobank]] |access-date=2017-08-11 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |author=印南敏秀 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |date=1993-12-28 |pages=375–376 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=高嶋賢二 |script-title=ja:村境の大草履―四国西南部の事例を中心に |publisher=The Society for MINGU of Japan (日本民具学会) |script-journal=ja:民具研究 |volume=131 |year=2005 |pages=45-61 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=小口千明 |script-title=ja:農村集落における精神的ムラ境の諸相 ―茨城県桜村における虫送りと道切りを事例として― |script-journal=ja:城西人文研究 |publisher=[[Josai University]] |volume=12 |pages=37–51 |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/els/contents110000187832.pdf?id=ART0000550707 |date=1987-02-10 |language=ja}}</ref>
The custom called Kanjo Nawa can be found around [[Kinki Region]], especially in [[Wakasa, Fukui|Wakasa]], [[Fukui Prefecture]], in the east and south of [[Shiga Prefecture]], in [[Iga, Mie|Iga, Mie Prefecture]], in the east mountainous region of [[Nara Prefecture]], and in Minami-Yamashiro of [[Kyoto Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite book |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |author=印南敏秀 |date=1993-12-28 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |page=377 |language=ja}}</ref> Kanjo Nawa is also known as '''Kanjo Tsuri''' (勧請吊).<ref>{{cite journal |author=原田敏丸 |year=1958 |script-title=ja:近江の勧請吊 彦根論叢 |url=http://libdspace.biwako.shiga-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10441/7726/2/HIKONE%20RONSO_048-049_345-360Y%20harada.pdf |script-journal=ja:人文科学特集第9号合併 |publisher=Economic Society of [[Shiga University]] (滋賀大学経済学会) |volume=48/49 |pages=345–360 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Examples == *Kanjo Nawa in Asukaji, [[Kasagi, Kyoto]] *:In Asukaji, in every January, villagers create Shimenawa, which is stretched over Nunome-Gawa River, running through the village, in order to pray for prosperity and safety. One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as [[Cultural Property (Japan)|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] in 1998.<ref>{{cite book | title=京都の文化財第16集 | url=http://www.kyoto-be.ne.jp/bunkazai/cms/?action=common_download_main&upload_id=1280 | year=1999 | pages=33–34 | publisher=[[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefectural]] Board of Education| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|access-date=2017-09-11}}</ref>
<gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|Kanjo Nawa stretched over the river in Asukaji File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|The stage of Amaterasumikado Shrine (天照御門神社) in Kasagi, where Kanjo Nawa is created. </gallery>

It was recognized as [[Cultural Property (Japan)|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] in 1998.<ref>{{cite book | title=京都の文化財第16集 | url=http://www.kyoto-be.ne.jp/bunkazai/cms/?action=common_download_main&upload_id=1280 | year=1999 | pages=33–34 | publisher=[[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefectural]] Board of Education| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|access-date=2017-09-11}}</ref>
