[[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Japanese religious terminology]] [[en:Kanjo Nawa]][[en:Kanjo Nawa]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:qq 5]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Kanjo Nawa]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Kanjo Nawa]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] {{Japan-culture-stub}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category:]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kanjo Nawa]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kanjo Nawa]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]]

'''Kanjo Nawa''' ({{lang-ja|勧請縄}}) is a Japanese custom of stretching [[shimenawa]], a variety of laid rope, with [[Fetishism#Japan|fetishes]] hung at the border of a village. {{nihongo|{{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り}}|道切り}} is just a similar custom. The term Kanjo Nawa also refers to the rope itself.
In rural area around Japan, there remains the custom of enshrining the items such as ropes of straw and grass, dolls, and straw sandals at the border of the village intended to prevent the evil such as an epidemic from entering the village, or to drive out the evil.<ref name="KOTOBANK">{{cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8B%A7%E8%AB%8B%E7%B8%84-1295507 |script-title=ja:勧請縄(かんじょうなわ)とは – コトバンク |publisher=[[:ja:コトバンク|Kotobank]] |access-date=2017-08-11 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |author=印南敏秀 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |date=1993-12-28 |pages=375–376 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=高嶋賢二 |script-title=ja:村境の大草履―四国西南部の事例を中心に |publisher=The Society for MINGU of Japan (日本民具学会) |script-journal=ja:民具研究 |volume=131 |year=2005 |pages=45-61 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=小口千明 |script-title=ja:農村集落における精神的ムラ境の諸相 ―茨城県桜村における虫送りと道切りを事例として― |script-journal=ja:城西人文研究 |publisher=[[Josai University]] |volume=12 |pages=37–51 |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/els/contents110000187832.pdf?id=ART0000550707 |date=1987-02-10 |language=ja}}</ref>
The custom called Kanjo Nawa can be found around [[Kinki Region]], especially in [[Wakasa, Fukui|Wakasa]], [[Fukui Prefecture]], in the east and south of [[Shiga Prefecture]], in [[Iga, Mie|Iga, Mie Prefecture]], in the east mountainous region of [[Nara Prefecture]], and in Minami-Yamashiro of [[Kyoto Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite book |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |author=印南敏秀 |date=1993-12-28 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |page=377 |language=ja}}</ref> Kanjo Nawa is also known as '''Kanjo Tsuri''' (勧請吊).<ref>{{cite journal |author=原田敏丸 |year=1958 |script-title=ja:近江の勧請吊 彦根論叢 |url=http://libdspace.biwako.shiga-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10441/7726/2/HIKONE%20RONSO_048-049_345-360Y%20harada.pdf |script-journal=ja:人文科学特集第9号合併 |publisher=Economic Society of [[Shiga University]] (滋賀大学経済学会) |volume=48/49 |pages=345–360 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Examples == *Kanjo Nawa in Asukaji, [[Kasagi, Kyoto]] *:In Asukaji, in every January, villagers create Shimenawa, which is stretched over Nunome-Gawa River, running through the village, in order to pray for prosperity and safety. One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as [[Cultural Property (Japan)|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] in 1998.<ref>{{cite book | title=京都の文化財第16集 | url=http://www.kyoto-be.ne.jp/bunkazai/cms/?action=common_download_main&upload_id=1280 | year=1999 | pages=33–34 | publisher=[[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefectural]] Board of Education| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|access-date=2017-09-11}}</ref>
<gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|Kanjo Nawa stretched over the river in Asukaji File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|The stage of Amaterasumikado Shrine (天照御門神社) in Kasagi, where Kanjo Nawa is created. </gallery>

'''Kanjo Nawa''' ({{lang-ja|勧請縄}}) is a Japanese custom of stretching [[shimenawa]], a variety of laid rope, with [[Fetishism#Japan|fetishes]] hung at the border of a village. {{nihongo|{{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り}}|道切り}} is just a similar custom. The term Kanjo Nawa also refers to the rope itself.
In rural area around Japan, there remains the custom of enshrining the items such as ropes of straw and grass, dolls, and straw sandals at the border of the village intended to prevent the evil such as an epidemic from entering the village, or to drive out the evil.<ref name="KOTOBANK">{{cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8B%A7%E8%AB%8B%E7%B8%84-1295507 |script-title=ja:勧請縄(かんじょうなわ)とは – コトバンク |publisher=[[:ja:コトバンク|Kotobank]] |access-date=2017-08-11 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |author=印南敏秀 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |date=1993-12-28 |pages=375–376 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=高嶋賢二 |script-title=ja:村境の大草履―四国西南部の事例を中心に |publisher=The Society for MINGU of Japan (日本民具学会) |script-journal=ja:民具研究 |volume=131 |year=2005 |pages=45-61 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=小口千明 |script-title=ja:農村集落における精神的ムラ境の諸相 ―茨城県桜村における虫送りと道切りを事例として― |script-journal=ja:城西人文研究 |publisher=[[Josai University]] |volume=12 |pages=37–51 |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/els/contents110000187832.pdf?id=ART0000550707 |date=1987-02-10 |language=ja}}</ref>
The custom called Kanjo Nawa can be found around [[Kinki Region]], especially in [[Wakasa, Fukui|Wakasa]], [[Fukui Prefecture]], in the east and south of [[Shiga Prefecture]], in [[Iga, Mie|Iga, Mie Prefecture]], in the east mountainous region of [[Nara Prefecture]], and in Minami-Yamashiro of [[Kyoto Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite book |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |author=印南敏秀 |date=1993-12-28 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |page=377 |language=ja}}</ref> Kanjo Nawa is also known as '''Kanjo Tsuri''' (勧請吊).<ref>{{cite journal |author=原田敏丸 |year=1958 |script-title=ja:近江の勧請吊 彦根論叢 |url=http://libdspace.biwako.shiga-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10441/7726/2/HIKONE%20RONSO_048-049_345-360Y%20harada.pdf |script-journal=ja:人文科学特集第9号合併 |publisher=Economic Society of [[Shiga University]] (滋賀大学経済学会) |volume=48/49 |pages=345–360 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Examples == *Kanjo Nawa in Asukaji, [[Kasagi, Kyoto]] *:In Asukaji, in every January, villagers create Shimenawa, which is stretched over Nunome-Gawa River, running through the village, in order to pray for prosperity and safety. One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as [[Cultural Property (Japan)|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] in 1998.<ref>{{cite book | title=京都の文化財第16集 | url=http://www.kyoto-be.ne.jp/bunkazai/cms/?action=common_download_main&upload_id=1280 | year=1999 | pages=33–34 | publisher=[[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefectural]] Board of Education| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|access-date=2017-09-11}}</ref>
<gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|Kanjo Nawa stretched over the river in Asukaji File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|The stage of Amaterasumikado Shrine (天照御門神社) in Kasagi, where Kanjo Nawa is created. </gallery>

The custom called Kanjo Nawa can be found around [[Kinki Region]], especially in [[Wakasa, Fukui|Wakasa]], [[Fukui Prefecture]], in the east and south of [[Shiga Prefecture]], in [[Iga, Mie|Iga, Mie Prefecture]], in the east mountainous region of [[Nara Prefecture]], and in Minami-Yamashiro of [[Kyoto Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite book |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |author=印南敏秀 |date=1993-12-28 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |page=377 |language=ja}}</ref> Kanjo Nawa is also known as '''Kanjo Tsuri''' (勧請吊).<ref>{{cite journal |author=原田敏丸 |year=1958 |script-title=ja:近江の勧請吊 彦根論叢 |url=http://libdspace.biwako.shiga-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10441/7726/2/HIKONE%20RONSO_048-049_345-360Y%20harada.pdf |script-journal=ja:人文科学特集第9号合併 |publisher=Economic Society of [[Shiga University]] (滋賀大学経済学会) |volume=48/49 |pages=345–360 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Examples == *Kanjo Nawa in Asukaji, [[Kasagi, Kyoto]] *:In Asukaji, in every January, villagers create Shimenawa, which is stretched over Nunome-Gawa River, running through the village, in order to pray for prosperity and safety. One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as [[Cultural Property (Japan)|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] in 1998.<ref>{{cite book | title=京都の文化財第16集 | url=http://www.kyoto-be.ne.jp/bunkazai/cms/?action=common_download_main&upload_id=1280 | year=1999 | pages=33–34 | publisher=[[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefectural]] Board of Education| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|access-date=2017-09-11}}</ref>
<gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|Kanjo Nawa stretched over the river in Asukaji File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|The stage of Amaterasumikado Shrine (天照御門神社) in Kasagi, where Kanjo Nawa is created. </gallery>
== See also == * {{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り}} – Similar custom * [[Shimenawa]]

'''Kanjo Nawa''' ({{lang-ja|勧請縄}}) is a Japanese custom of stretching [[shimenawa]], a variety of laid rope, with [[Fetishism#Japan|fetishes]] hung at the border of a village. {{nihongo|{{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り}}|道切り}} is just a similar custom. The term Kanjo Nawa also refers to the rope itself.
In rural area around Japan, there remains the custom of enshrining the items such as ropes of straw and grass, dolls, and straw sandals at the border of the village intended to prevent the evil such as an epidemic from entering the village, or to drive out the evil.<ref name="KOTOBANK">{{cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8B%A7%E8%AB%8B%E7%B8%84-1295507 |script-title=ja:勧請縄(かんじょうなわ)とは – コトバンク |publisher=[[:ja:コトバンク|Kotobank]] |access-date=2017-08-11 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |author=印南敏秀 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |date=1993-12-28 |pages=375–376 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=高嶋賢二 |script-title=ja:村境の大草履―四国西南部の事例を中心に |publisher=The Society for MINGU of Japan (日本民具学会) |script-journal=ja:民具研究 |volume=131 |year=2005 |pages=45-61 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=小口千明 |script-title=ja:農村集落における精神的ムラ境の諸相 ―茨城県桜村における虫送りと道切りを事例として― |script-journal=ja:城西人文研究 |publisher=[[Josai University]] |volume=12 |pages=37–51 |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/els/contents110000187832.pdf?id=ART0000550707 |date=1987-02-10 |language=ja}}</ref>
The custom called Kanjo Nawa can be found around [[Kinki Region]], especially in [[Wakasa, Fukui|Wakasa]], [[Fukui Prefecture]], in the east and south of [[Shiga Prefecture]], in [[Iga, Mie|Iga, Mie Prefecture]], in the east mountainous region of [[Nara Prefecture]], and in Minami-Yamashiro of [[Kyoto Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite book |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |author=印南敏秀 |date=1993-12-28 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |page=377 |language=ja}}</ref> Kanjo Nawa is also known as '''Kanjo Tsuri''' (勧請吊).<ref>{{cite journal |author=原田敏丸 |year=1958 |script-title=ja:近江の勧請吊 彦根論叢 |url=http://libdspace.biwako.shiga-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10441/7726/2/HIKONE%20RONSO_048-049_345-360Y%20harada.pdf |script-journal=ja:人文科学特集第9号合併 |publisher=Economic Society of [[Shiga University]] (滋賀大学経済学会) |volume=48/49 |pages=345–360 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Examples == *Kanjo Nawa in Asukaji, [[Kasagi, Kyoto]] *:In Asukaji, in every January, villagers create Shimenawa, which is stretched over Nunome-Gawa River, running through the village, in order to pray for prosperity and safety. One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as [[Cultural Property (Japan)|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] in 1998.<ref>{{cite book | title=京都の文化財第16集 | url=http://www.kyoto-be.ne.jp/bunkazai/cms/?action=common_download_main&upload_id=1280 | year=1999 | pages=33–34 | publisher=[[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefectural]] Board of Education| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|access-date=2017-09-11}}</ref>
<gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|Kanjo Nawa stretched over the river in Asukaji File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|The stage of Amaterasumikado Shrine (天照御門神社) in Kasagi, where Kanjo Nawa is created. </gallery>
== See also == * {{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り}} – Similar custom * [[Shimenawa]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External links == * [http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452 Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)] – explanation by the local government of Kasagi Town
{{Japan-culture-stub}} {{Shinto shrine}}

One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. The term Kanjo Nawa also refers to the rope itself.
In rural area around Japan, there remains the custom of enshrining the items such as ropes of straw and grass, dolls, and straw sandals at the border of the village intended to prevent the evil such as an epidemic from entering the village, or to drive out the evil.<ref name="KOTOBANK">{{cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8B%A7%E8%AB%8B%E7%B8%84-1295507 |script-title=ja:勧請縄(かんじょうなわ)とは – コトバンク |publisher=[[:ja:コトバンク|Kotobank]] |access-date=2017-08-11 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |author=印南敏秀 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |date=1993-12-28 |pages=375–376 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=高嶋賢二 |script-title=ja:村境の大草履―四国西南部の事例を中心に |publisher=The Society for MINGU of Japan (日本民具学会) |script-journal=ja:民具研究 |volume=131 |year=2005 |pages=45-61 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=小口千明 |script-title=ja:農村集落における精神的ムラ境の諸相 ―茨城県桜村における虫送りと道切りを事例として― |script-journal=ja:城西人文研究 |publisher=[[Josai University]] |volume=12 |pages=37–51 |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/els/contents110000187832.pdf?id=ART0000550707 |date=1987-02-10 |language=ja}}</ref>

One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as [[Cultural Property (Japan)|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] in 1998.<ref>{{cite book | title=京都の文化財第16集 | url=http://www.kyoto-be.ne.jp/bunkazai/cms/?action=common_download_main&upload_id=1280 | year=1999 | pages=33–34 | publisher=[[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefectural]] Board of Education| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|access-date=2017-09-11}}</ref>

{{draft categories| }}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Japanese religious terminology]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:5]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Kanjo Nawa]] [[Category:New]] }} [[en:Kanjo Nawa]] [[sn:Kanjo Nawa]]

'''Kanjo Nawa''' ({{lang-ja|勧請縄}}) is a Japanese custom of stretching [[shimenawa]], a variety of laid rope, with [[Fetishism#Japan|fetishes]] hung at the border of a village. {{nihongo|{{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り|12=draft|12=draft}}|道切り}} is just a similar custom. The term Kanjo Nawa also refers to the rope itself.
In rural area around Japan, there remains the custom of enshrining the items such as ropes of straw and grass, dolls, and straw sandals at the border of the village intended to prevent the evil such as an epidemic from entering the village, or to drive out the evil.<ref name="KOTOBANK">{{cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8B%A7%E8%AB%8B%E7%B8%84-1295507 |script-title=ja:勧請縄(かんじょうなわ)とは – コトバンク |publisher=[[:ja:コトバンク|Kotobank]] |access-date=2017-08-11 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |author=印南敏秀 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |date=1993-12-28 |pages=375–376 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=高嶋賢二 |script-title=ja:村境の大草履―四国西南部の事例を中心に |publisher=The Society for MINGU of Japan (日本民具学会) |script-journal=ja:民具研究 |volume=131 |year=2005 |pages=45-61 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=小口千明 |script-title=ja:農村集落における精神的ムラ境の諸相 ―茨城県桜村における虫送りと道切りを事例として― |script-journal=ja:城西人文研究 |publisher=[[Josai University]] |volume=12 |pages=37–51 |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/els/contents110000187832.pdf?id=ART0000550707 |date=1987-02-10 |language=ja}}</ref>
The custom called Kanjo Nawa can be found around [[Kinki Region]], especially in [[Wakasa, Fukui|Wakasa]], [[Fukui Prefecture]], in the east and south of [[Shiga Prefecture]], in [[Iga, Mie|Iga, Mie Prefecture]], in the east mountainous region of [[Nara Prefecture]], and in Minami-Yamashiro of [[Kyoto Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite book |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |author=印南敏秀 |date=1993-12-28 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |page=377 |language=ja}}</ref> Kanjo Nawa is also known as '''Kanjo Tsuri''' (勧請吊).<ref>{{cite journal |author=原田敏丸 |year=1958 |script-title=ja:近江の勧請吊 彦根論叢 |url=http://libdspace.biwako.shiga-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10441/7726/2/HIKONE%20RONSO_048-049_345-360Y%20harada.pdf |script-journal=ja:人文科学特集第9号合併 |publisher=Economic Society of [[Shiga University]] (滋賀大学経済学会) |volume=48/49 |pages=345–360 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Examples == *Kanjo Nawa in Asukaji, [[Kasagi, Kyoto]] *:In Asukaji, in every January, villagers create Shimenawa, which is stretched over Nunome-Gawa River, running through the village, in order to pray for prosperity and safety. One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as [[Cultural Property (Japan)|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] in 1998.<ref>{{cite book | title=京都の文化財第16集 | url=http://www.kyoto-be.ne.jp/bunkazai/cms/?action=common_download_main&upload_id=1280 | year=1999 | pages=33–34 | publisher=[[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefectural]] Board of Education| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|access-date=2017-09-11}}</ref>
<gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|Kanjo Nawa stretched over the river in Asukaji File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|The stage of Amaterasumikado Shrine (天照御門神社) in Kasagi, where Kanjo Nawa is created. </gallery>
== See also == * {{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り|12=draft|12=draft}} – Similar custom * [[Shimenawa]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External links == * [http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452 Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)] – explanation by the local government of Kasagi Town

<gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|Kanjo Nawa stretched over the river in Asukaji File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|The stage of Amaterasumikado Shrine (天照御門神社) in Kasagi, where Kanjo Nawa is created. </gallery>

revision.comment: Bot: add simplewiki draft link to ill templates
revision.text:
'''Kanjo Nawa''' ({{lang-ja|勧請縄}}) is a Japanese custom of stretching [[shimenawa]], a variety of laid rope, with [[Fetishism#Japan|fetishes]] hung at the border of a village. {{nihongo|{{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Michi Kiri|qq=}}|道切り}} is just a similar custom. The term Kanjo Nawa also refers to the rope itself.
In rural area around Japan, there remains the custom of enshrining the items such as ropes of straw and grass, dolls, and straw sandals at the border of the village intended to prevent the evil such as an epidemic from entering the village, or to drive out the evil.<ref name="KOTOBANK">{{cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8B%A7%E8%AB%8B%E7%B8%84-1295507 |script-title=ja:勧請縄(かんじょうなわ)とは – コトバンク |publisher=[[:ja:コトバンク|Kotobank]] |access-date=2017-08-11 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |author=印南敏秀 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |date=1993-12-28 |pages=375–376 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=高嶋賢二 |script-title=ja:村境の大草履―四国西南部の事例を中心に |publisher=The Society for MINGU of Japan (日本民具学会) |script-journal=ja:民具研究 |volume=131 |year=2005 |pages=45-61 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=小口千明 |script-title=ja:農村集落における精神的ムラ境の諸相 ―茨城県桜村における虫送りと道切りを事例として― |script-journal=ja:城西人文研究 |publisher=[[Josai University]] |volume=12 |pages=37–51 |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/els/contents110000187832.pdf?id=ART0000550707 |date=1987-02-10 |language=ja}}</ref>
The custom called Kanjo Nawa can be found around [[Kinki Region]], especially in [[Wakasa, Fukui|Wakasa]], [[Fukui Prefecture]], in the east and south of [[Shiga Prefecture]], in [[Iga, Mie|Iga, Mie Prefecture]], in the east mountainous region of [[Nara Prefecture]], and in Minami-Yamashiro of [[Kyoto Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite book |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |author=印南敏秀 |date=1993-12-28 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |page=377 |language=ja}}</ref> Kanjo Nawa is also known as '''Kanjo Tsuri''' (勧請吊).<ref>{{cite journal |author=原田敏丸 |year=1958 |script-title=ja:近江の勧請吊 彦根論叢 |url=http://libdspace.biwako.shiga-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10441/7726/2/HIKONE%20RONSO_048-049_345-360Y%20harada.pdf |script-journal=ja:人文科学特集第9号合併 |publisher=Economic Society of [[Shiga University]] (滋賀大学経済学会) |volume=48/49 |pages=345–360 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Examples == *Kanjo Nawa in Asukaji, [[Kasagi, Kyoto]] *:In Asukaji, in every January, villagers create Shimenawa, which is stretched over Nunome-Gawa River, running through the village, in order to pray for prosperity and safety. One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as [[Cultural Property (Japan)|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] in 1998.<ref>{{cite book | title=京都の文化財第16集 | url=http://www.kyoto-be.ne.jp/bunkazai/cms/?action=common_download_main&upload_id=1280 | year=1999 | pages=33–34 | publisher=[[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefectural]] Board of Education| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|access-date=2017-09-11}}</ref>
<gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|Kanjo Nawa stretched over the river in Asukaji File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|The stage of Amaterasumikado Shrine (天照御門神社) in Kasagi, where Kanjo Nawa is created. </gallery>

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q30925247]] Wikidata name: Kanjo Nawa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q30925247]] Wikidata name: Kanjo Nawa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kanjo Nawa''' ({{lang-ja|勧請縄}}) is a Japanese custom of stretching [[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]], a variety of laid rope, with [[Fetishism#Japan|fetishes]] hung at the border of a village. {{nihongo|{{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Michi Kiri|qq=}}|道切り}} is just a similar custom. The term Kanjo Nawa also refers to the rope itself.
In rural area around Japan, there remains the custom of enshrining the items such as ropes of straw and grass, dolls, and straw sandals at the border of the village intended to prevent the evil such as an epidemic from entering the village, or to drive out the evil.<ref name="KOTOBANK">{{cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8B%A7%E8%AB%8B%E7%B8%84-1295507 |script-title=ja:勧請縄(かんじょうなわ)とは – コトバンク |publisher=[[:ja:コトバンク|Kotobank]] |access-date=2017-08-11 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |author=印南敏秀 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |date=1993-12-28 |pages=375–376 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=高嶋賢二 |script-title=ja:村境の大草履―四国西南部の事例を中心に |publisher=The Society for MINGU of Japan (日本民具学会) |script-journal=ja:民具研究 |volume=131 |year=2005 |pages=45-61 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=小口千明 |script-title=ja:農村集落における精神的ムラ境の諸相 ―茨城県桜村における虫送りと道切りを事例として― |script-journal=ja:城西人文研究 |publisher=[[Josai University]] |volume=12 |pages=37–51 |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/els/contents110000187832.pdf?id=ART0000550707 |date=1987-02-10 |language=ja}}</ref>
The custom called Kanjo Nawa can be found around [[:en:Kinki Region|Kinki Region]], especially in [[:en:Wakasa, Fukui|Wakasa]], [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]], in the east and south of [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]], in [[:en:Iga, Mie|Iga, Mie Prefecture]], in the east mountainous region of [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]], and in Minami-Yamashiro of [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite book |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |author=印南敏秀 |date=1993-12-28 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |page=377 |language=ja}}</ref> Kanjo Nawa is also known as '''Kanjo Tsuri''' (勧請吊).<ref>{{cite journal |author=原田敏丸 |year=1958 |script-title=ja:近江の勧請吊 彦根論叢 |url=http://libdspace.biwako.shiga-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10441/7726/2/HIKONE%20RONSO_048-049_345-360Y%20harada.pdf |script-journal=ja:人文科学特集第9号合併 |publisher=Economic Society of [[Shiga University]] (滋賀大学経済学会) |volume=48/49 |pages=345–360 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Examples == *Kanjo Nawa in Asukaji, [[:en:Kasagi, Kyoto|Kasagi, Kyoto]] *:In Asukaji, in every January, villagers create Shimenawa, which is stretched over Nunome-Gawa River, running through the village, in order to pray for prosperity and safety. One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as [[:en:Cultural Property (Japan)|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] in 1998.<ref>{{cite book | title=京都の文化財第16集 | url=http://www.kyoto-be.ne.jp/bunkazai/cms/?action=common_download_main&upload_id=1280 | year=1999 | pages=33–34 | publisher=[[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefectural]] Board of Education| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|access-date=2017-09-11}}</ref>
<gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|Kanjo Nawa stretched over the river in Asukaji File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|The stage of Amaterasumikado Shrine (天照御門神社) in Kasagi, where Kanjo Nawa is created. </gallery>
== See also == * {{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Michi Kiri|qq=}} – Similar custom * [[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]]

== External links == * [http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452 Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)] – explanation by the local government of Kasagi Town

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kanjo Nawa]][[ja:勧請縄]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q30925247]] Wikidata name: Kanjo Nawa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q30925247]] Wikidata name: Kanjo Nawa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kanjo Nawa''' ({{lang-ja|勧請縄}}) is a Japanese custom of stretching [[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]], a variety of laid rope, with [[Fetishism#Japan|fetishes]] hung at the border of a village. {{nihongo|{{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Michi Kiri|1=michikiri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11641628}}|道切り}} is just a similar custom. The term Kanjo Nawa also refers to the rope itself.
In rural area around Japan, there remains the custom of enshrining the items such as ropes of straw and grass, dolls, and straw sandals at the border of the village intended to prevent the evil such as an epidemic from entering the village, or to drive out the evil.<ref name="KOTOBANK">{{cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8B%A7%E8%AB%8B%E7%B8%84-1295507 |script-title=ja:勧請縄(かんじょうなわ)とは – コトバンク |publisher=[[:ja:コトバンク|Kotobank]] |access-date=2017-08-11 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |author=印南敏秀 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |date=1993-12-28 |pages=375–376 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=高嶋賢二 |script-title=ja:村境の大草履―四国西南部の事例を中心に |publisher=The Society for MINGU of Japan (日本民具学会) |script-journal=ja:民具研究 |volume=131 |year=2005 |pages=45-61 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=小口千明 |script-title=ja:農村集落における精神的ムラ境の諸相 ―茨城県桜村における虫送りと道切りを事例として― |script-journal=ja:城西人文研究 |publisher=[[Josai University]] |volume=12 |pages=37–51 |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/els/contents110000187832.pdf?id=ART0000550707 |date=1987-02-10 |language=ja}}</ref>
The custom called Kanjo Nawa can be found around [[:en:Kinki Region|Kinki Region]], especially in [[:en:Wakasa, Fukui|Wakasa]], [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]], in the east and south of [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]], in [[:en:Iga, Mie|Iga, Mie Prefecture]], in the east mountainous region of [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]], and in Minami-Yamashiro of [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite book |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |author=印南敏秀 |date=1993-12-28 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |page=377 |language=ja}}</ref> Kanjo Nawa is also known as '''Kanjo Tsuri''' (勧請吊).<ref>{{cite journal |author=原田敏丸 |year=1958 |script-title=ja:近江の勧請吊 彦根論叢 |url=http://libdspace.biwako.shiga-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10441/7726/2/HIKONE%20RONSO_048-049_345-360Y%20harada.pdf |script-journal=ja:人文科学特集第9号合併 |publisher=Economic Society of [[Shiga University]] (滋賀大学経済学会) |volume=48/49 |pages=345–360 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Examples == *Kanjo Nawa in Asukaji, [[:en:Kasagi, Kyoto|Kasagi, Kyoto]] *:In Asukaji, in every January, villagers create Shimenawa, which is stretched over Nunome-Gawa River, running through the village, in order to pray for prosperity and safety. One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as [[:en:Cultural Property (Japan)|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] in 1998.<ref>{{cite book | title=京都の文化財第16集 | url=http://www.kyoto-be.ne.jp/bunkazai/cms/?action=common_download_main&upload_id=1280 | year=1999 | pages=33–34 | publisher=[[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefectural]] Board of Education| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|access-date=2017-09-11}}</ref>
<gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|Kanjo Nawa stretched over the river in Asukaji File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|The stage of Amaterasumikado Shrine (天照御門神社) in Kasagi, where Kanjo Nawa is created. </gallery>
== See also == * {{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Michi Kiri|1=michikiri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11641628}} – Similar custom * [[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kanjo Nawa]][[ja:勧請縄]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q30925247]] Wikidata name: Kanjo Nawa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q30925247]] Wikidata name: Kanjo Nawa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kanjo Nawa''' ({{lang-ja|勧請縄}}) is a Japanese custom of stretching [[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]], a variety of laid rope, with [[Fetishism#Japan|fetishes]] hung at the border of a village. {{nihongo|{{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Michi Kiri|1=michikiri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11641628}}|道切り}} is just a similar custom. The term Kanjo Nawa also refers to the rope itself.
In rural area around Japan, there remains the custom of enshrining the items such as ropes of straw and grass, dolls, and straw sandals at the border of the village intended to prevent the evil such as an epidemic from entering the village, or to drive out the evil.<ref name="KOTOBANK">{{cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8B%A7%E8%AB%8B%E7%B8%84-1295507 |script-title=ja:勧請縄(かんじょうなわ)とは – コトバンク |publisher=[[:ja:コトバンク|Kotobank]] |access-date=2017-08-11 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |author=印南敏秀 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |date=1993-12-28 |pages=375–376 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=高嶋賢二 |script-title=ja:村境の大草履―四国西南部の事例を中心に |publisher=The Society for MINGU of Japan (日本民具学会) |script-journal=ja:民具研究 |volume=131 |year=2005 |pages=45-61 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=小口千明 |script-title=ja:農村集落における精神的ムラ境の諸相 ―茨城県桜村における虫送りと道切りを事例として― |script-journal=ja:城西人文研究 |publisher=[[Josai University]] |volume=12 |pages=37–51 |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/els/contents110000187832.pdf?id=ART0000550707 |date=1987-02-10 |language=ja}}</ref>
The custom called Kanjo Nawa can be found around [[:en:Kinki Region|Kinki Region]], especially in [[:en:Wakasa, Fukui|Wakasa]], [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]], in the east and south of [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]], in [[:en:Iga, Mie|Iga, Mie Prefecture]], in the east mountainous region of [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]], and in Minami-Yamashiro of [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite book |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |author=印南敏秀 |date=1993-12-28 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |page=377 |language=ja}}</ref> Kanjo Nawa is also known as '''Kanjo Tsuri''' (勧請吊).<ref>{{cite journal |author=原田敏丸 |year=1958 |script-title=ja:近江の勧請吊 彦根論叢 |url=http://libdspace.biwako.shiga-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10441/7726/2/HIKONE%20RONSO_048-049_345-360Y%20harada.pdf |script-journal=ja:人文科学特集第9号合併 |publisher=Economic Society of [[Shiga University]] (滋賀大学経済学会) |volume=48/49 |pages=345–360 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Examples == *Kanjo Nawa in Asukaji, [[:en:Kasagi, Kyoto|Kasagi, Kyoto]] *:In Asukaji, in every January, villagers create Shimenawa, which is stretched over Nunome-Gawa River, running through the village, in order to pray for prosperity and safety. One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as [[:en:Cultural Property (Japan)|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] in 1998.<ref>{{cite book | title=京都の文化財第16集 | url=http://www.kyoto-be.ne.jp/bunkazai/cms/?action=common_download_main&upload_id=1280 | year=1999 | pages=33–34 | publisher=[[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefectural]] Board of Education| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|access-date=2017-09-11}}</ref>
<gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|Kanjo Nawa stretched over the river in Asukaji File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|The stage of Amaterasumikado Shrine (天照御門神社) in Kasagi, where Kanjo Nawa is created. </gallery>
== See also == * {{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Michi Kiri|1=michikiri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11641628}} – Similar custom * [[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kanjo Nawa]][[ja:勧請縄]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q30925247]] Wikidata name: Kanjo Nawa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q30925247]] Wikidata name: Kanjo Nawa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kanjo Nawa''' ({{lang-ja|勧請縄}}) is a Japanese custom of stretching [[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]], a variety of laid rope, with [[Fetishism#Japan|fetishes]] hung at the border of a village. {{nihongo|{{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Michi Kiri|1=michikiri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11641628}}|道切り}} is just a similar custom. The term Kanjo Nawa also refers to the rope itself.
In rural area around Japan, there remains the custom of enshrining the items such as ropes of straw and grass, dolls, and straw sandals at the border of the village intended to prevent the evil such as an epidemic from entering the village, or to drive out the evil.<ref name="KOTOBANK">{{cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8B%A7%E8%AB%8B%E7%B8%84-1295507 |script-title=ja:勧請縄(かんじょうなわ)とは – コトバンク |publisher=[[:ja:コトバンク|Kotobank]] |access-date=2017-08-11 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |author=印南敏秀 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |date=1993-12-28 |pages=375–376 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=高嶋賢二 |script-title=ja:村境の大草履―四国西南部の事例を中心に |publisher=The Society for MINGU of Japan (日本民具学会) |script-journal=ja:民具研究 |volume=131 |year=2005 |pages=45-61 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=小口千明 |script-title=ja:農村集落における精神的ムラ境の諸相 ―茨城県桜村における虫送りと道切りを事例として― |script-journal=ja:城西人文研究 |publisher=[[Josai University]] |volume=12 |pages=37–51 |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/els/contents110000187832.pdf?id=ART0000550707 |date=1987-02-10 |language=ja}}</ref>
The custom called Kanjo Nawa can be found around [[:en:Kinki Region|Kinki Region]], especially in [[:en:Wakasa, Fukui|Wakasa]], [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]], in the east and south of [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]], in [[:en:Iga, Mie|Iga, Mie Prefecture]], in the east mountainous region of [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]], and in Minami-Yamashiro of [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite book |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |author=印南敏秀 |date=1993-12-28 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |page=377 |language=ja}}</ref> Kanjo Nawa is also known as '''Kanjo Tsuri''' (勧請吊).<ref>{{cite journal |author=原田敏丸 |year=1958 |script-title=ja:近江の勧請吊 彦根論叢 |url=http://libdspace.biwako.shiga-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10441/7726/2/HIKONE%20RONSO_048-049_345-360Y%20harada.pdf |script-journal=ja:人文科学特集第9号合併 |publisher=Economic Society of [[Shiga University]] (滋賀大学経済学会) |volume=48/49 |pages=345–360 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Examples == *Kanjo Nawa in Asukaji, [[:en:Kasagi, Kyoto|Kasagi, Kyoto]] *:In Asukaji, in every January, villagers create Shimenawa, which is stretched over Nunome-Gawa River, running through the village, in order to pray for prosperity and safety. One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as [[:en:Cultural Property (Japan)|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] in 1998.<ref>{{cite book | title=京都の文化財第16集 | url=http://www.kyoto-be.ne.jp/bunkazai/cms/?action=common_download_main&upload_id=1280 | year=1999 | pages=33–34 | publisher=[[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefectural]] Board of Education| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|access-date=2017-09-11}}</ref>
<gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|Kanjo Nawa stretched over the river in Asukaji File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|The stage of Amaterasumikado Shrine (天照御門神社) in Kasagi, where Kanjo Nawa is created. </gallery>
== See also == * {{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Michi Kiri|1=michikiri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11641628}} – Similar custom * [[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External links == * [http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452 Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)] – explanation by the local government of Kasagi Town
{{Japan-culture-stub}} <!--this was an inappropriate and erroneous transclusion meant to be a bottom bar thing I think but not including-->
{{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Japanese religious terminology]]
}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kanjo Nawa]][[ja:勧請縄]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kanjo Nawa]][[ja:勧請縄]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kanjo Nawa]][[ja:勧請縄]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kanjo Nawa]][[ja:勧請縄]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q30925247]] Wikidata name: Kanjo Nawa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q30925247]] Wikidata name: Kanjo Nawa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kanjo Nawa''' ({{lang-ja|勧請縄}}) is a Japanese custom of stretching [[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]], a variety of laid rope, with [[Fetishism#Japan|fetishes]] hung at the border of a village. {{nihongo|{{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Michi Kiri|1=michikiri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11641628}}|道切り}} is just a similar custom. The term Kanjo Nawa also refers to the rope itself.
In rural area around Japan, there remains the custom of enshrining the items such as ropes of straw and grass, dolls, and straw sandals at the border of the village intended to prevent the evil such as an epidemic from entering the village, or to drive out the evil.<ref name="KOTOBANK">{{cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8B%A7%E8%AB%8B%E7%B8%84-1295507 |script-title=ja:勧請縄(かんじょうなわ)とは – コトバンク |publisher=[[:ja:コトバンク|Kotobank]] |access-date=2017-08-11 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |author=印南敏秀 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |date=1993-12-28 |pages=375–376 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=高嶋賢二 |script-title=ja:村境の大草履―四国西南部の事例を中心に |publisher=The Society for MINGU of Japan (日本民具学会) |script-journal=ja:民具研究 |volume=131 |year=2005 |pages=45-61 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=小口千明 |script-title=ja:農村集落における精神的ムラ境の諸相 ―茨城県桜村における虫送りと道切りを事例として― |script-journal=ja:城西人文研究 |publisher=[[Josai University]] |volume=12 |pages=37–51 |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/els/contents110000187832.pdf?id=ART0000550707 |date=1987-02-10 |language=ja}}</ref>
The custom called Kanjo Nawa can be found around [[:en:Kinki Region|Kinki Region]], especially in [[:en:Wakasa, Fukui|Wakasa]], [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]], in the east and south of [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]], in [[:en:Iga, Mie|Iga, Mie Prefecture]], in the east mountainous region of [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]], and in Minami-Yamashiro of [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite book |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |author=印南敏秀 |date=1993-12-28 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |page=377 |language=ja}}</ref> Kanjo Nawa is also known as '''Kanjo Tsuri''' (勧請吊).<ref>{{cite journal |author=原田敏丸 |year=1958 |script-title=ja:近江の勧請吊 彦根論叢 |url=http://libdspace.biwako.shiga-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10441/7726/2/HIKONE%20RONSO_048-049_345-360Y%20harada.pdf |script-journal=ja:人文科学特集第9号合併 |publisher=Economic Society of [[Shiga University]] (滋賀大学経済学会) |volume=48/49 |pages=345–360 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Examples == *Kanjo Nawa in Asukaji, [[:en:Kasagi, Kyoto|Kasagi, Kyoto]] *:In Asukaji, in every January, villagers create Shimenawa, which is stretched over Nunome-Gawa River, running through the village, in order to pray for prosperity and safety. One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as [[:en:Cultural Property (Japan)|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] in 1998.<ref>{{cite book | title=京都の文化財第16集 | url=http://www.kyoto-be.ne.jp/bunkazai/cms/?action=common_download_main&upload_id=1280 | year=1999 | pages=33–34 | publisher=[[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefectural]] Board of Education| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|access-date=2017-09-11}}</ref>
<gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|Kanjo Nawa stretched over the river in Asukaji File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|The stage of Amaterasumikado Shrine (天照御門神社) in Kasagi, where Kanjo Nawa is created. </gallery>
== See also == * {{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Michi Kiri|1=michikiri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11641628}} – Similar custom * [[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External links == * [http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452 Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)] – explanation by the local government of Kasagi Town

[[en:Kanjo Nawa]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ja:勧請縄]] {{wikidata link|Q30925247}} [[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Japanese religious terminology]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kanjo Nawa]][[ja:勧請縄]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kanjo Nawa]][[ja:勧請縄]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kanjo Nawa]][[ja:勧請縄]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kanjo Nawa]][[ja:勧請縄]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q30925247]] Wikidata name: Kanjo Nawa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q30925247]] Wikidata name: Kanjo Nawa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kanjo Nawa''' ({{lang-ja|勧請縄}}) is a Japanese custom of stretching [[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]], a variety of laid rope, with [[Fetishism#Japan|fetishes]] hung at the border of a village. {{nihongo|{{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Michi Kiri|1=michikiri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11641628}}|道切り}} is just a similar custom. The term Kanjo Nawa also refers to the rope itself.
In rural area around Japan, there remains the custom of enshrining the items such as ropes of straw and grass, dolls, and straw sandals at the border of the village intended to prevent the evil such as an epidemic from entering the village, or to drive out the evil.<ref name="KOTOBANK">{{cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8B%A7%E8%AB%8B%E7%B8%84-1295507 |script-title=ja:勧請縄(かんじょうなわ)とは – コトバンク |publisher=[[:ja:コトバンク|Kotobank]] |access-date=2017-08-11 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |author=印南敏秀 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |date=1993-12-28 |pages=375–376 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=高嶋賢二 |script-title=ja:村境の大草履―四国西南部の事例を中心に |publisher=The Society for MINGU of Japan (日本民具学会) |script-journal=ja:民具研究 |volume=131 |year=2005 |pages=45-61 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=小口千明 |script-title=ja:農村集落における精神的ムラ境の諸相 ―茨城県桜村における虫送りと道切りを事例として― |script-journal=ja:城西人文研究 |publisher=[[Josai University]] |volume=12 |pages=37–51 |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/els/contents110000187832.pdf?id=ART0000550707 |date=1987-02-10 |language=ja}}</ref>
The custom called Kanjo Nawa can be found around [[:en:Kinki Region|Kinki Region]], especially in [[:en:Wakasa, Fukui|Wakasa]], [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]], in the east and south of [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]], in [[:en:Iga, Mie|Iga, Mie Prefecture]], in the east mountainous region of [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]], and in Minami-Yamashiro of [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite book |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |author=印南敏秀 |date=1993-12-28 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |page=377 |language=ja}}</ref> Kanjo Nawa is also known as '''Kanjo Tsuri''' (勧請吊).<ref>{{cite journal |author=原田敏丸 |year=1958 |script-title=ja:近江の勧請吊 彦根論叢 |url=http://libdspace.biwako.shiga-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10441/7726/2/HIKONE%20RONSO_048-049_345-360Y%20harada.pdf |script-journal=ja:人文科学特集第9号合併 |publisher=Economic Society of [[Shiga University]] (滋賀大学経済学会) |volume=48/49 |pages=345–360 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Examples == *Kanjo Nawa in Asukaji, [[:en:Kasagi, Kyoto|Kasagi, Kyoto]] *:In Asukaji, in every January, villagers create Shimenawa, which is stretched over Nunome-Gawa River, running through the village, in order to pray for prosperity and safety. One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as [[:en:Cultural Property (Japan)|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] in 1998.<ref>{{cite book | title=京都の文化財第16集 | url=http://www.kyoto-be.ne.jp/bunkazai/cms/?action=common_download_main&upload_id=1280 | year=1999 | pages=33–34 | publisher=[[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefectural]] Board of Education| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|access-date=2017-09-11}}</ref>
<gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|Kanjo Nawa stretched over the river in Asukaji File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|The stage of Amaterasumikado Shrine (天照御門神社) in Kasagi, where Kanjo Nawa is created. </gallery>
== See also == * {{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Michi Kiri|1=michikiri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11641628}} – Similar custom * [[:en:Shimenawa|Shimenawa]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External links == * [http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452 Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)] – explanation by the local government of Kasagi Town

The custom called Kanjo Nawa can be found around [[:en:Kinki Region|Kinki Region]], especially in {{ill|Wakasa, Fukui|en|Wakasa, Fukui|WD=Q1349039|lt=Wakasa}}, {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture}}, in the east and south of {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|WD=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture}}, in {{ill|Iga, Mie|en|Iga, Mie|WD=Q460820|lt=Iga, Mie Prefecture}}, in the east mountainous region of {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}}, and in Minami-Yamashiro of {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}}.<ref>{{cite book |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |author=印南敏秀 |date=1993-12-28 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |page=377 |language=ja}}</ref> Kanjo Nawa is also known as '''Kanjo Tsuri''' (勧請吊).<ref>{{cite journal |author=原田敏丸 |year=1958 |script-title=ja:近江の勧請吊 彦根論叢 |url=http://libdspace.biwako.shiga-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10441/7726/2/HIKONE%20RONSO_048-049_345-360Y%20harada.pdf |script-journal=ja:人文科学特集第9号合併 |publisher=Economic Society of [[Shiga University]] (滋賀大学経済学会) |volume=48/49 |pages=345–360 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Examples == *Kanjo Nawa in Asukaji, {{ill|Kasagi, Kyoto|en|Kasagi, Kyoto|WD=Q1203404|lt=Kasagi, Kyoto}} *:In Asukaji, in every January, villagers create Shimenawa, which is stretched over Nunome-Gawa River, running through the village, in order to pray for prosperity and safety. One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as {{ill|Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q858308|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties}} in 1998.<ref>{{cite book | title=京都の文化財第16集 | url=http://www.kyoto-be.ne.jp/bunkazai/cms/?action=common_download_main&upload_id=1280 | year=1999 | pages=33–34 | publisher={{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefectural}} Board of Education| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|access-date=2017-09-11}}</ref>
<gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|Kanjo Nawa stretched over the river in Asukaji File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|The stage of Amaterasumikado Shrine (天照御門神社) in Kasagi, where Kanjo Nawa is created. </gallery>

[[en:Kanjo Nawa]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ja:勧請縄]] {{wikidata link|Q30925247}} [[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Japanese religious terminology]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

revision.comment: adding dummy category to make categories work
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kanjo Nawa]][[ja:勧請縄]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kanjo Nawa]][[ja:勧請縄]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kanjo Nawa]][[ja:勧請縄]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kanjo Nawa]][[ja:勧請縄]]
'''Kanjo Nawa''' ({{lang-ja|勧請縄}}) is a Japanese custom of stretching {{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|WD=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa}}, a variety of laid rope, with [[Fetishism#Japan|fetishes]] hung at the border of a village. {{nihongo|{{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Michi Kiri|1=michikiri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11641628}}|道切り}} is just a similar custom. The term Kanjo Nawa also refers to the rope itself.
In rural area around Japan, there remains the custom of enshrining the items such as ropes of straw and grass, dolls, and straw sandals at the border of the village intended to prevent the evil such as an epidemic from entering the village, or to drive out the evil.<ref name="KOTOBANK">{{cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8B%A7%E8%AB%8B%E7%B8%84-1295507 |script-title=ja:勧請縄(かんじょうなわ)とは – コトバンク |publisher=[[:ja:コトバンク|Kotobank]] |access-date=2017-08-11 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |author=印南敏秀 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |date=1993-12-28 |pages=375–376 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=高嶋賢二 |script-title=ja:村境の大草履―四国西南部の事例を中心に |publisher=The Society for MINGU of Japan (日本民具学会) |script-journal=ja:民具研究 |volume=131 |year=2005 |pages=45-61 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=小口千明 |script-title=ja:農村集落における精神的ムラ境の諸相 ―茨城県桜村における虫送りと道切りを事例として― |script-journal=ja:城西人文研究 |publisher=[[Josai University]] |volume=12 |pages=37–51 |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/els/contents110000187832.pdf?id=ART0000550707 |date=1987-02-10 |language=ja}}</ref>
The custom called Kanjo Nawa can be found around [[:en:Kinki Region|Kinki Region]], especially in {{ill|Wakasa, Fukui|en|Wakasa, Fukui|WD=Q1349039|lt=Wakasa}}, {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture}}, in the east and south of {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|WD=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture}}, in {{ill|Iga, Mie|en|Iga, Mie|WD=Q460820|lt=Iga, Mie Prefecture}}, in the east mountainous region of {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}}, and in Minami-Yamashiro of {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}}.<ref>{{cite book |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |author=印南敏秀 |date=1993-12-28 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |page=377 |language=ja}}</ref> Kanjo Nawa is also known as '''Kanjo Tsuri''' (勧請吊).<ref>{{cite journal |author=原田敏丸 |year=1958 |script-title=ja:近江の勧請吊 彦根論叢 |url=http://libdspace.biwako.shiga-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10441/7726/2/HIKONE%20RONSO_048-049_345-360Y%20harada.pdf |script-journal=ja:人文科学特集第9号合併 |publisher=Economic Society of [[Shiga University]] (滋賀大学経済学会) |volume=48/49 |pages=345–360 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Examples == *Kanjo Nawa in Asukaji, {{ill|Kasagi, Kyoto|en|Kasagi, Kyoto|WD=Q1203404|lt=Kasagi, Kyoto}} *:In Asukaji, in every January, villagers create Shimenawa, which is stretched over Nunome-Gawa River, running through the village, in order to pray for prosperity and safety. One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as {{ill|Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q858308|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties}} in 1998.<ref>{{cite book | title=京都の文化財第16集 | url=http://www.kyoto-be.ne.jp/bunkazai/cms/?action=common_download_main&upload_id=1280 | year=1999 | pages=33–34 | publisher={{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefectural}} Board of Education| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|access-date=2017-09-11}}</ref>
<gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|Kanjo Nawa stretched over the river in Asukaji File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|The stage of Amaterasumikado Shrine (天照御門神社) in Kasagi, where Kanjo Nawa is created. </gallery>
== See also == * {{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Michi Kiri|1=michikiri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11641628}} – Similar custom * {{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|WD=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa}}

== External links == * [http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452 Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)] – explanation by the local government of Kasagi Town

== Examples == *Kanjo Nawa in Asukaji, {{ill|Kasagi, Kyoto|en|Kasagi, Kyoto|WD=Q1203404|lt=Kasagi, Kyoto|1=Kasagi, Kyoto}} *:In Asukaji, in every January, villagers create Shimenawa, which is stretched over Nunome-Gawa River, running through the village, in order to pray for prosperity and safety. One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as {{ill|Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q858308|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|1=Cultural Property (Japan)}} in 1998.<ref>{{cite book | title=京都の文化財第16集 | url=http://www.kyoto-be.ne.jp/bunkazai/cms/?action=common_download_main&upload_id=1280 | year=1999 | pages=33–34 | publisher={{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefectural|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} Board of Education| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|access-date=2017-09-11}}</ref>
<gallery> File:笠置町飛鳥路勧請縄03.jpg|Kanjo Nawa stretched over the river in Asukaji File:AmaterasuMikadoShrine-butai.jpg|The stage of Amaterasumikado Shrine (天照御門神社) in Kasagi, where Kanjo Nawa is created. </gallery>

[[en:Kanjo Nawa]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ja:勧請縄]] {{wikidata link|Q30925247}} [[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Japanese religious terminology]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kanjo Nawa]][[ja:勧請縄]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kanjo Nawa]][[ja:勧請縄]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kanjo Nawa]][[ja:勧請縄]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kanjo Nawa]][[ja:勧請縄]]
'''Kanjo Nawa''' ({{lang-ja|勧請縄}}) is a Japanese custom of stretching {{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|qid=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa|1=Shimenawa}}, a variety of laid rope, with [[Fetishism#Japan|fetishes]] hung at the border of a village. {{nihongo|{{ill|Michi Kiri|ja|道切り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Michi Kiri|1=michikiri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11641628}}|道切り}} is just a similar custom. The term Kanjo Nawa also refers to the rope itself.
In rural area around Japan, there remains the custom of enshrining the items such as ropes of straw and grass, dolls, and straw sandals at the border of the village intended to prevent the evil such as an epidemic from entering the village, or to drive out the evil.<ref name="KOTOBANK">{{cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%8B%A7%E8%AB%8B%E7%B8%84-1295507 |script-title=ja:勧請縄(かんじょうなわ)とは – コトバンク |publisher=[[:ja:コトバンク|Kotobank]] |access-date=2017-08-11 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |author=印南敏秀 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |date=1993-12-28 |pages=375–376 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=高嶋賢二 |script-title=ja:村境の大草履―四国西南部の事例を中心に |publisher=The Society for MINGU of Japan (日本民具学会) |script-journal=ja:民具研究 |volume=131 |year=2005 |pages=45-61 |language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=小口千明 |script-title=ja:農村集落における精神的ムラ境の諸相 ―茨城県桜村における虫送りと道切りを事例として― |script-journal=ja:城西人文研究 |publisher=[[Josai University]] |volume=12 |pages=37–51 |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/els/contents110000187832.pdf?id=ART0000550707 |date=1987-02-10 |language=ja}}</ref>
The custom called Kanjo Nawa can be found around [[:en:Kinki Region|Kinki Region]], especially in {{ill|Wakasa, Fukui|en|Wakasa, Fukui|qid=Q1349039|lt=Wakasa|1=Wakasa, Fukui}}, {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|qid=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture|1=Fukui Prefecture}}, in the east and south of {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|qid=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture|1=Shiga Prefecture}}, in {{ill|Iga, Mie|en|Iga, Mie|qid=Q460820|lt=Iga, Mie Prefecture|1=Iga, Mie}}, in the east mountainous region of {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|qid=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}, and in Minami-Yamashiro of {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|qid=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}.<ref>{{cite book |script-title=ja:民俗文化分布圏論 |author=印南敏秀 |date=1993-12-28 |isbn=4-626-01486-0 |editor=植木行宣 |editor2=樋口昭 |page=377 |language=ja}}</ref> Kanjo Nawa is also known as '''Kanjo Tsuri''' (勧請吊).<ref>{{cite journal |author=原田敏丸 |year=1958 |script-title=ja:近江の勧請吊 彦根論叢 |url=http://libdspace.biwako.shiga-u.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10441/7726/2/HIKONE%20RONSO_048-049_345-360Y%20harada.pdf |script-journal=ja:人文科学特集第9号合併 |publisher=Economic Society of [[Shiga University]] (滋賀大学経済学会) |volume=48/49 |pages=345–360 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Examples == *Kanjo Nawa in Asukaji, {{ill|Kasagi, Kyoto|en|Kasagi, Kyoto|qid=Q1203404|lt=Kasagi, Kyoto|1=Kasagi, Kyoto}} *:In Asukaji, in every January, villagers create Shimenawa, which is stretched over Nunome-Gawa River, running through the village, in order to pray for prosperity and safety. One person per household is supposed to attend the event. Tsukurimono (ツクリモノ), imitating farming equipment, daily-life instrument or sexual organs, is hung on Shimenawa. It was recognized as {{ill|Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Cultural Property (Japan)|qid=Q858308|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|1=Cultural Property (Japan)}} in 1998.<ref>{{cite book | title=京都の文化財第16集 | url=http://www.kyoto-be.ne.jp/bunkazai/cms/?action=common_download_main&upload_id=1280 | year=1999 | pages=33–34 | publisher={{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|qid=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefectural|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} Board of Education| language=ja}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.town.kasagi.lg.jp/contents_detail.php?frmId=452|title=Kanjo Nawa (Sacred Rope Protecting Local People From Evil Spirits)|publisher=[[Kasagi,_Kyoto|Kasagi Town]]|access-date=2017-09-11}}</ref>
